CYP2B6*18 (c. 983T>C, p.I328T) – No function: CYP2B6*18 is assigned no function (note that the formerly assigned CYP2B6*16 allele is now a suballele of CYP2B6*18). Several clinical studies indicate that CYP2B6*18 is strongly associated with higher plasma efavirenz exposure in patients with HIV (18281305, 23080225, 26655325). Studies evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among patients with HIV taking efavirenz for more than three months found that patients with the CYP2B6*18 allele had nearly double efavirenz exposure compared to the *1 reference (26655325, 34115651). Another study evaluating plasma efavirenz concentrations in patients with HIV found three-fold higher efavirenz concentrations in patients carrying CYP2B6*18 compared to the *1 reference (16495778). Furthermore, CYP2B6*18 is associated with significantly higher exposure of nevirapine in plasma (18281305, 25878720) and hair (34115651) and lower exposure of hydroxybupropion after bupropion administration (23344581). Lower clearance of methadone compared to the *1 reference was found in a CYP2B6*18 carrier (35538637). Several in vitro studies found that CYP2B6*18 exhibited undetectable (or markedly decreased) protein expression and/or activity towards different probe substrates including efavirenz, bupropion, ketamine, and methadone, in several cDNA expression systems (16272958, 16495778, 20517174, 21746968, 25897175, 30085944, 30201214, 32238417, 35538637). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on strong evidence.

CYP2B6*14 (c.419G>A, p.R140Q) - Uncertain function: CYP2B6*14 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using human liver microsomes (17559344, 15190123) and cDNA expression systems (15190123, 20517174, 30201214, 21746968) found that CYP2B6*14 has decreased enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference. However, due to the lack of any in vivo or clinical evidence, consensus among experts was uncertain clinical function based on limited evidence.

CYP2C9*28 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*28 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype. These studies used different cell systems and substrates included warfarin, tolbutamide, diclofenac, losartan, and glimepiride (23752738, 19541829, 16788382). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

name: *1x≥3
findings: CYP2D6*1x≥3 is assigned increased function based on limited evidence. Subjects with CYP2D6 gene duplications demonstrated increased enzyme activity with the greatest debrisoquine metabolism among 270 subjects based on urine metabolites (7616439). Additionally, debrisoquine metabolism correlated with the number of functional CYP2D6 genes (7616439, 9012401). Therefore, consensus among experts was increased function with an activity value of ≥3 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2B6*11 (c.136A>G, p.M46V) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*11 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems demonstrated decreased or no enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (20517174, 21746968, 15190123, 30201214). Some studies with heterozygous and homozygous CYP2B6*11 patients showed that this allele is associated with increased exposure of efavirenz (27045425, 26779253, 28886044), while other clinical studies (25303294, 23734829, 19916993, 23778320) did not consistently demonstrate decreased enzyme activity or altered PK with efavirenz. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on consistent in vitro data but limited and inconsistent clinical evidence.

CYP2D6*144 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*144 is defined by a splice accepter variant predicting abberant splicing. A subject sequenced as CYP2D6*143/*144 had a risperidone/9-hydroxy-risperidone ratio that was higher (indicating lower activity) compared to that found in subjects with two no function alleles. Two subjects genotyped as *1/*144 and *2/*144 had ratios comparable to that of subjects with an AS of 1 (33998274). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function.

CYP2D6*145 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. CYP2D6*145 is defined by the insertion of three amino acids which also define CYP2D6*30; *145 lacks two others also present in *30. In vitro studies demonstrated decreased enzyme activity for *30 compared to *1 with tamoxifen, bufuralol, dextromethorphan, and primaquine substrates (24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function.

NAT2*21 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. NAT2*21 is characterized by the core SNVs c.458C>T (p.T153I) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown normal function in vivo and in vitro. Published in silico modeling of c.458C>T (p.T153I) predicts that this variant impairs protein function based on the exposure of hydrophobic residues that may facilitate protein aggregation and/or targeted protein degradation (19909761). However, nine out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.458C>T (p.T153I) is neutral for NAT2 protein function and structure-based tools predict that this variant may increase protein stability.

TPMT*16 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*16 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (15226673, 15652243, 33118454). In vitro studies found that TPMT.16 exhibited decreased affinity for 6-TG and enzymatic activity was decreased by 84% and 68% relative to TPMT.1 in mammalian (18708949) and yeast (15652243) cells, respectively. An in silico prediction model of TPMT variant functionality can predict >95% of variance when including TPMT*16 along with the most common variants TPMT*2, *3A, and *3C, compared to 70% without TPMT*16 (32973300). Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*16-defining SNV, c.488G>A (p.R163H), impairs protein function.

NAT2*38 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*38 is characterized by the core SNVs c.590G>A (p.R197Q), c.766A>G (p.K256E) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. Published in silico modeling predicts that c.766A>G (p.K256E) does not alter protein function (19909761). Four out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.766A>G impairs protein function. c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*38 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. While c.766A>G is not predicted to impair protein function, it remains unknown whether there are any combinatorial effects associated with c.590G>A and c.766A>G.

CYP2D6*137 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate literature describing function of this allele. One in vitro study demonstrated decreased intrinsic clearance CLint (Vmax/Km) of 50.1% and 78.2% compared to *1 for tamoxifen N-desmethylation and dextromethorphan O-demethylation, respectively (32759992). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

NAT2*73 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*73 is defined by the core SNVs c.341T>C (p.I114T) and c.802A>G (p.R268G). c.341T>C (p.I114T) defines NAT2*5 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. In vitro studies also show that NAT2*5 (which contains the core SNV c.341T>C, p.I114T) is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*73 would presumably be degraded in the same manner. Additionally, five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C (p.I114T) impairs protein function. There is no literature describing function for c.802A>G. Three out of eight variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.802A>G (p.R268G) impairs protein function. NAT2*73 is assigned decreased function based on c.341T>C. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.341T>C and c.802A>G are uncertain.

CYP2C19*13 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. Three in vitro studies using different cell systems found CYP2C19*13 demonstrated similar enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, omeprazole, clopidogrel and other substrates (12464799, 21325430, 25001882, 21692664). Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically similar to phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2C9*19 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. One subject with CYP2C9*1/*19 genotype exhibited decreased clearance compared to wildtype subjects based on tolbutamide plasma concentrations and urine metabolite concentrations after one 500mg dose. This result correlated with the in vitro study where CYP2C9*19 demonstrated 31% enzyme activity with a tolbutamide substrate compared to wildtype (25614704). Multiple in vitro studies similarly found CYP2C9*19 demonstrated decreased clearance with various substrates compared to wildtype (23752738, 23844998, 24118918, 25142737, 25476996, 25144335, 25951663, 25832633, 25884291, 27163851, 24077631), however, one in vitro study showed CYP2C9*19 had similar activity compared to wildtype with a tolbutamide substrate (16099926). Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned but experts agreed additional evidence may change the assignment to decreased function.

NUDT15*12 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. An in vitro study expressed NUDT15*12 in HEK293 cells and found there was no effect on thiopurine sensitivity (32094176). An additional in vitro study found that NUDT15.12 exhibits decreased protein thermostability compared to NUDT15.1 (29519865). Patients carrying the NUDT15*12 allele had a 20% reduction in thiopurine dosing after the occurrence of adverse reactions (29519865). Seven out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the NUDT15*12-defining SNV (c.156C>G (p.F52L)) impairs protein function.

TPMT*19 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*19 exhibited normal activity (15652243). In an in vitro study using a yeast expression system, TPMT.19 activity was decreased by 30% relative to TPMT.1 using 6-TG as a substrate (15652243). Another in vitro study using a mammalian expression system found TPMT.19 enzymatic activity was decreased by 40% compared to TPMT.1 (18708949). Six out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*19-defining SNV, c.365A>C (p.K122T), impairs protein function.

TPMT*22 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*22 exhibited intermediate activity (16917910). An in vitro study using a mammalian cell expression system found that TPMT.22 protein expression and enzymatic activity was undetectable (18708949). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*22-defining SNV, c.488G>C (p.R163P), impairs protein function.

CYP2D6*128 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate literature describing function of this allele. One in vitro study demonstrated substantially decreased intrinsic clearance CLint (Vmax/Km) of 3.3% compared to *1 for tamoxifen N-desmethylation (32759992); no activity was detectable for dextromethorphan O-demethylation. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

NUDT15*7 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. Patients carrying a NUDT15*7 allele tolerate lower doses of thiopurines (28659275 , 32873882 , 32221476 ). An in vitro study using a bacterial expression system found that NUDT15.7 enzymatic activity was undetectable and NUDT15.7 exhibited decreased protein thermostability compared to NUDT15.1 (28659275). An additional in vitro study expressed NUDT15*7 in HEK293 cells and found that the protein was expressed at similar levels to NUDT15.1, but cells were significantly more sensitive to thiopurine treatment (32094176). Nine out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the NUDT15*7-defining SNV, c.101G>C (p.R34T), impairs protein function.

NUDT15*11 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. An in vitro study expressed NUDT15*11 in HEK293 cells and found that protein expression and thiopurine sensitivity were comparable to cells expressing NUDT15*1 (32094176). Nine out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the NUDT15*11-defining SNV (c.139G>A (p.G47R)) impairs protein function.

TPMT*23 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*3A/*23 or *3C/*23 exhibited low activity (17885628). An in vitro study using a mammalian cell expression system found that TPMT.23 exhibited decreased affinity for 6-TG and TPMT.23 enzymatic activity is decreased by 40% compared to TPMT.1 (18708949). Published in silico modeling suggests that TPMT.23 exhibited decreased protein stability as well as altered substrate and co-factor binding (17885628, 23025308). Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*23-defining SNV, c.500C>G (p.A167G), impairs protein function.

SLCO1B1*4 is assigned uncertain function based on in vitro studies demonstrating unaltered uptake of estrone sulfate, estradiol 17beta-d-glucuronide and rifampin (11477075, 12490595) and normal protein expression (33658230), but no clinical data. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

CYP2C19*38 is assigned normal function based on definitive evidence. CYP2C19*38 was originally catalogued as CYP2C19*1.001 (i.e. CYP2C19*1A) (2009263). Due to the standardized nomenclature system this allele received its own star designation and is now catalogued as CYP2C19*38 (32602114). Therefore, because CYP2C19*38 does not contain any single nucleotide polymorphisms and the new star designation was a result of nomenclature standardization, consensus among experts was normal function.

SLCO1B1*8 is assigned uncertain function based on in vitro studies demonstrating unaltered uptake of estrone sulfate, estradiol 17beta-d-glucuronide and rifampin (11477075, 12490595), but no clinical data. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*36 (c.-82T>C, c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R) – Decreased function: CYB2B6*36 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*36 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information) (23418033). As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2B6*41 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R; c.1138T>C, p.Y380H) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*41 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*41 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information). As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). A single in vitro study reported decreased function for p.Y380H using buproprion and efavirez as probes (38596098). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2D6*132 is assigned decreased function with an activity value of 0.25 due to limited literature describing function of this allele. One in vitro study demonstrated substantially decreased intrinsic clearance CLint (Vmax/Km) of 3.8% and 0.5% compared to *1 for tamoxifen N-desmethylation and dextromethorphan O-demethylation, respectively (32759992). The allele contains 100C>T and 4181G>C (present in *10) and 2819A>G. There are no data describing the consequence of 2819A>G; however, based on the function of *10 and the in vivo study, consensus among experts was decreased function due to limited evidence.

CYP2C19*5 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence in heterozygous subjects and in vitro studies. CYP2C19*5 was first described in a subject demonstrating decreased mephenytoin metabolism compared to wildtype subjects and similar mephenytoin metabolism compared to CYP2C19 *2/*2, CYP2C19 *2/*3, and CYP2C19 *3/*3 subjects based on S/R mephenytoin ratio in urine metabolites (9103550) . One CYP2C19*2/*5 subject similarly demonstrated decreased phenytoin metabolism based on S/R mephenytoin ratio in urine metabolites. This result was correlated with an in vitro study which showed CYP2C19*5 had a 40% decrease in enzyme expression, 0.4% enzyme activity with a mephenytoin substrate, and nondetectable enzyme activity with a tolbutamide substrate compared to wildtype (10022751). Two additional in vitro studies using different cell systems found CYP2C19*5 had nondetectable enzyme activity with mephenytoin, omeprazole, and clopidogrel substrates (21325430, 25001882). CYP2C19.5B showed no activity towards CEC (3-cyano-7-ethoxycoumarin) O-deethylation (21692664). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on moderate evidence.

CYP2C9*26 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*26 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype. These studies used different cell systems and substrates included warfarin, tolbutamide, diclofenac, losartan, and glimepiride (23752738, 19541829, 16788382). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

TPMT*26 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. An individual genotyped TPMT*1/*26 experienced hematological toxicity after standard azathioprine dosing and erythrocytes from the patient and two siblings also harboring TPMT*26 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (19660010). Four out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*26-defining SNV, c.622T>C (p.F208L), impairs protein function.

CYP2D6*15 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*15 was first described in a subject demonstrating markedly decreased dextromethorphan and debrisoquine metabolism (8807669). CYP2D6*15 is defined by an amino acid insertion resulting in missense sequence and premature stop codon, yielding a truncated nonfunctional enzyme. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

clinicalfunctionalstatus: increased risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR1 1555A>G is assigned an increased risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on definitive evidence. Screening of individuals or probands of individuals with aminoglycoside-hearing loss identified 1494C>T in a number of cases (7689389, 8285309, 8414970, 9039999, 9040738, 9111378, 9164619, 9391883, 9490575, 9779807, 9831149, 9950117, 10633132, 10661905, 11174959, 11857751, 11870684, 12031626, 12920080, 14699607, 14755216, 15708009, 15841390, 15917167, 16168391, 16375862, 16458854, 16935512, 16955413, 17341440, 17637808, 17723226, 17999439, 18386806, 18790089, 18820594, 18830133, 19376484, 19818876, 19835846, 20100600, 20111055, 20353758, 21162657, 21205314, 21504270, 22475488, 23256547, 24252789, 25744662, 26497601, 27397648, 27427311, 29805548, 24703164, 25155176). There is inhibiton of translation in fibroblasts and cybrid cell lines with 1555A>G mutation (8687424). Aminoglycosides bind to 12S rRNA carrying the G allele, but not to rRNA with the A allele (9315872, 12127547, 19687236, 28103015). LCLs with the 1555A>G mutation have reduced growth and translation when grown in paromomycin (10915767). No effect of 1555A>G mutation in fibroblast lines. Cybrids with 1555A>G had slower growth in neomycin or paromomycin (12054632, 21828074). Therefore, consensus among experts was this variant is associated with increased risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on definitive evidence.

One study in human liver microsomes found that CYP2B6*22 had increased enzyme activity towards efavirenz and bupropion compared to the *1 reference (17559344). A second study found increased levels of CYP2B6*22 mRNA expression levels in human liver tissue and enzyme activity in liver microsomes compared to the *1 reference (15722458). CYP2C9*4 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence in heterozygous patients and in vitro studies. One patient on a stable dose of warfarin with CYP2C9*1/*4 genotype required a lower weekly warfarin dose compared to CYP2C9*1/*1 patients (21228733). In an in vitro study CYP2C9*4 demonstrated 16% enzyme activity with a warfarin substrate and 22% enzyme activity with a tolbutamide substrate compared to wildtype. The CYP2C9*4 defining SNP occurs in the substrate recognition site which likely contributes to the loss of enzyme activity (23752738). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2D6*59 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. CYP2D6*59 has been described in subjects demonstrating decreased sparteine and dextromethorphan metabolism (17001296, 26335396). An in vitro study found CYP2D6*59 exhibited decreased mRNA levels and enzyme activity with a propafenone substrate compared to wildtype (17001296). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on moderate evidence.

name: rs2231142 variant (T)
findings: 421C>A is assigned a decreased function based strong evidence with numerous in vitro and clinical studies supporting decreased function. It has been found to decrease the expression of BCRP (25236865, 21091277, 22098950, 18464048, 18958403). Expression of the BCRP protein was reported to be 30–40% that of protein with the reference sequence, and expression in the placenta, specifically, was also reportedly lower (15475413, 15553238). The c.421C>A variant has been found to affect the pharmacokinetics of, response to, and toxicity of compounds that are BCRP substrates, including chemotherapeutics and endogenous compounds (10646850, 11688982, 11309344, 25639366, 26914831). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with strong evidence.

CYP2C9*40 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence in one heterozygous subject and in vitro studies. One subject with CYP2C9 *1/*40 genotype exhibited decreased AUC and half-life but similar oral clearance compared to wildtype subjects based on tolbutamide plasma concentrations after one 500mg dose (25614704). In vitro studies found CYP2C9*40 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from decreased to increased compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 23844998, 25142737, 25144335, 25951663, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25924705, 27163851, 28758225, 25614704). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

NAT2*54 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. NAT2*54 is characterized by the core SNVs c.29T>C (p.I10T) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. Published in silico modeling predicts that c.29T>C (p.I10T) does not alter NAT2 protein function (19909761). Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.29T>C (p.I10T) impairs NAT2 protein function. Additionally, a structure-based computational prediction tool predicts that c.29T>C (p.I10T) decreases NAT2 protein stability which could lead to decreased function.

An in vitro study found CYP2C19*22 had 8% enzyme activity compared to wildtype with a clopidogrel substrate and nondetectable enzyme activity compared to wildtype with a mephenytoin substrate (25001882). Crystal structure analysis shows the Arg186Pro substitution in CYP2C19*22 disrupts hydrogen bonds and alters protein structure resulting in decreased heme affinity and ultimately enzyme activity (19164093). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function and decreased function alleles.

CYP2C9*62 is assigned uncertain funciton due to inadequate evidence. One in vitro study found the protein expression of CYP2C9*62 was significantly lower than that of the wild-type, and its metabolic activity was also significantly decreased after the addition of three typical CYP2C9 probe drugs (tolbutaminde, losartan, and diclofenac), suggesting that the new mutant can dramatically affect the metabolism of CYP2C9 drugs in vitro (32531309). Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

Two in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems provided inconclusive evidence for CYP2B6*35 function (23418033, 30201214). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence.

CYP2C9*31 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies using different cell systems found CYP2C9*31 demonstrated decreased clearance for numerous substrates compared to wildtype (23752738, 24118918, 23844998, 25144335, 25951663, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 27163851, 25924705, 27199745, 24077631). Although one in vitro study showed CYP2C9*31 had similar activity compared to wildtype, the experts did not find this result convincing as in this study CYP2C9*2 and CYP2C9*3 which definitively have decreased function and no function, respectively, demonstrated increased activity compared to wildtype (25142737). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

Based on evidence supporting the function of rs4149056C>T (11477075, 32961594, 21245207), SLCO1B1*40 is assigned uncertain function due to no evidence.

CYP2B6*31 (c.937C>A, p.L313I) – Normal function: CYP2B6*31 is assigned normal function. A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among patients with HIV taking efavirenz for more than three months found CYP2B6*31 did not impact long term efavirenz content (26655325). An in vitro study using a cDNA expression system similarly found CYP2B6*31 demonstrated similar enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (30201214). Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on limited evidence.

CYP2C9*46 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence in one heterozygous patient and in vitro studies. One subject with CYP2C9*1/*46 genotype exhibited increased AUC and decreased oral clearance compared to wildtype subjects based on tolbutamide plasma concentrations after one 500mg dose (25614704). Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*46 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25951663, 25924705, 23844998, 25614704). Although two in vitro studies showed CYP2C9*46 had similar activity compared to wildtype, the experts did not find these results convincing as in these studies CYP2C9*2 which definitively has decreased function, demonstrated increased or similar activity compared to wildtype in these studies (25142737, 27163851). An additional in vitro study showed CYP2C9*46 had similar activity compared to wildtype (25144335), however, the experts did not find this result convincing as the function of CYP2C9*46 in vivo and in vitro demonstrated decreased function toward the majority of substrates. Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2D6*35 is assigned normal function based on moderate evidence in heterozygous subjects. CYP2D6*35 was first identified in a subject with CYP2D6*5/*35 genotype demonstrating similar dextromethorphan metabolism compared to CYP2D6 *1/*5 subjects, indicating normal function of the CYP2D6*35 allele (9241659). Similarly, a subject with CYP2D6*4/*35 genotype demonstrated decreased dextromethorphan metabolism compared to wildtype subjects (21833166). Additionally, a study of 396 subjects found the -1584C>G substitution, which is found in CYP2D6*35 and other normal function alleles such as CYP2D6*2, was not observed in any subject demonstrating poor dextromethorphan metabolism, indicating normal function of the CYP2D6*35 allele (12766015). Although two in vitro studies found CYP2D6*35 had decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype (24647041, 30366777), experts did not find these results convincing as the expression system may not be reflective of in vivo environment as demonstrated by the numerous subjects carrying the CYP2D6*35 allele with substrate metabolism similar to that of wildtype as described previously. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function with an activity value of 1 based on moderate evidence.

name: Seattle, Lodi, Modena, Ferrara II, Athens-like
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

NAT2*70 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. An individual heterozygous for NAT2*70 experienced drug-induced hepatotoxicity after standard isoniazid dosing (35588539). NAT2*70 is defined by the core SNVs c.341T>C (p.I114T) and c.596T>C (p.I199T). c.341T>C (p.I114T) defines NAT2*5 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. In vitro studies also show that NAT2*5 (which contains the core SNV c.341T>C, p.I114T) is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*70 would presumably be degraded in the same manner. Additionally, five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C (p.I114T) impairs protein function. Three out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.596T>C (p.I199T) impairs protein function. NAT2*70 is assigned decreased function based on c.341T>C. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.341T>C and c.596T>C are uncertain.

CYP2B6*28 (c.917C>G, p.T306S; c.1132C>T, p.R378X) – No function: CYP2B6*28 is assigned no function. CYP2B6*28 is defined by a nonsense mutation which results in a premature stop codon resulting in a truncated protein. One study evaluating efavirenz plasma concentrations in patients with HIV found a subject with CYP2B6 *6/*28 genotype had elevated efavirenz AUC compared to the *1 reference and CYP2B6 *6/*6 subjects (17235330). In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found that CYP2B6*28 demonstrated no enzyme activity (17235330, 20517174). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence.

CYP2D6*25 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*25 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with tamoxifen, bufuralol, dextromethorphan, and primaquine substrates (24647041, 30366777). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

name: Cassano
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

NUDT15*9 is assigned no function based on strong evidence. An in vitro study using a bacterial expression system found that NUDT15.9 enzymatic activity was undetectable and NUDT15.9 exhibited decreased protein thermostability compared to NUDT15.1 (28659275). NUDT15*1/*9 patients exhibit decreased tolerance to thiopurines (28659275) and experience severe hematotoxicity (30728528, 30806694). NUDT15*9 is characterized by the deletion c.50delGAGTCG (p.del17_18GV), which is predicted to impair protein function by CADD and PhD-SNPg

CYP2D6*106 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. CYP2D6*106 is defined by a single SNP which in silico studies predict to be a benign amino acid change (26937359). An in vitro study found CYP2D6*106 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with bufuralol and dextromethorphan substrates (23897164). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

TPMT*4 is assigned no function based on strong evidence. The SNV characteristic of TPMT*4 (c.626-1G>A) is located in the splice junction between intron 9 and exon 10 and disrupts the canonical splice site, resulting in production of a truncated protein. Erythrocytes from individuals heterozygous for TPMT*4 exhibited intermediate TPMT enzymatic activity (9486974, 12880540), while erythrocytes from individuals carrying TPMT*4 in combination with another no function allele had very low TPMT enzymatic activity (9246020, 9486974). The in silico variant effect predictors, CADD and PhD-SNPg, predict that the TPMT*4-deifning SNV, c. 626-1G>A, impairs protein function. Additionally, c.626-1G>A is predicted to result in the loss of the acceptor splice site by SpliceAI.

CYP2D6*117 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

CYP2C19*8 is assigned no function based on limited evidence in a heterozygous subject and in vitro studies. CYP2C19*8 was first described in a subject demonstrating decreased metabolism of mephenytoin based on urine metabolite concentrations. This correlated with an in vitro study which found CYP2C19*8 had decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin and tolbutamide substrates (10411572). Two additional in vitro studies using different cell systems found CYP2C19*8 had nondetectable enzyme activity with mephenytoin, omeprazole, and clopidogrel substrates (21325430, 25001882). Another study found CYP2C19*8 exhibited significantly reduced catalytic activities compared with CYP2C19*1 across multiple substrates and distinct function from other decreased function alleles (21692664). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function and decreased function alleles.

CYP2D6*92 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*92 had nondetectable enzyme with various substrates (25469868, 26310775, 26666748, 26544071, 26406933, 27109434, 27097346, 27016952, 26542054, 26937172, 26968424, 27203132, 27251229, 27354764, 27538917). CYP2D6*92 is defined by a deletion resulting in a frameshift and early stop codon and yielding a truncated nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

CYP2D6*155 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. CYP2D6*155 is defined by the five SNPs defining the decreased function *29 allele and one additional SNP that is not found on any other star allele.

CYP2B6*13 (c.415A>G, p.K139E; c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R) - No function: CYP2B6*13 is assigned no function. CYP2B6*13 contains the same variants as CYP2B6*6, which is assigned decreased function, and CYP2B6*8 which is assigned no function. Most in vitro studies including those using cDNA expression systems (15190123, 20517174, 30201214) and human liver microsomes (17559344) found that CYP2B6*13 has no enzyme activity; only one in vitro study using a cDNA expression system showed residual activity for artemether metabolism compared to the *1 reference. This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence.

CYP2D6*142 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. A subject sequenced as CYP2D6*10/*142 had a risperidone/9-hydroxy-risperidone ratio comparable to subjects with an AS of 0.25, 0.5 or 0.75 (33998274). CYP2D6*142 is a rare allele defined by several SNPs including 100C>T which causes decreased function in other CYP2D6 alleles (e.g. *10). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function.

CYP2C19*25 is assigned decreased function based on limited in vitro and computational evidence. An in vitro study found CYP2C19*25 had decreased intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with clopidogrel and mephenytoin, and omeprazole substrates (25001882, 31713493). A computational analysis of the Phe448Leu amino acid substitution in CYP2C19*25 predicted the change as deleterious. An examination of the structure showed the amino acid substitution, which is important for the heme-binding region, could disrupt the hydrophobic pocket in the region (19636337). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

functioncomments: UGT1A1*80 is in very high linkage disequilibrium with *28 and *37. However, the function of the *80 allele alone, in the absence of *28 or *37, is uncertain. If a patient is only tested for *80, decreased function may be inferred.

findings: CYP2D6*3 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*3 was first described in a heterozygous subject demonstrating decreased sparteine metabolism based on an elevated urinary metabolite ratio. CYP2D6*3 is defined by a single nucleotide deletion causing a frameshift and resulting in a nonfunctional protein (2211621). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

CYP2D6*87 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. CYP2C9*35 is assigned no function based on limited evidence in one heterozygous subject and in vitro studies. CYP2C9*35 was first described in a patient requiring a very low weekly warfarin dose of 2.25mg (2145143). No variant was observed in VKORC1 or CYP4F2 in this patient and a subsequent in silico study showed a decreased interaction with reductase resulting in impaired enzyme activity. In vitro studies similarly found CYP2C9*35 demonstrated no enzyme activity with warfarin, tolbutamide, and diclofenac substrates (23752738, 24322786). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

CYP2D6*40 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence in heterozygous subjects and in vitro studies. CYP2D6*40 is defined by an 18 base pair insertion. CYP2D6*40 has been described in subjects with CYP2D6 *4/*40, CYP2D6 *6/*40, and CYP2D6 *40/*42 genotype demonstrating poor metabolism with a dextromethorphan substrate (12152006, 12811367). Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*40 had nondetectable enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on moderate evidence.

CYP2D6*56 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. A subject with CYP2D6 *4/*56 genotype demonstrated poor dextromethorphan metabolism based on urine metabolites on multiple occasions (17392730). CYP2D6*56 was identified in an hepatocyte demonstrating undetectable CYP2D6 protein expression and undetectable enzyme activity with dextromethorphan and bufuralol substrates (16679388). CYP2D6*56 is defined by a premature stop codon yielding a truncated and nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

CYP2D6*68 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*68 is a CYP2D6-CYP2D7 hybrid gene similar to CYP2D6*4 which results in a nonfunctional protein (22111604). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

TPMT*11 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from a patient genotyped TPMT*3A/*11 exhibited very low enzymatic activity and 6-MMP was undetectable in the patient's plasma and their 6-MP dose was reduced to 15% of the standard dosage (12835738). In vitro studies using mammalian cell expression systems found TPMT.11 protein expression is decreased (16220112). TPMT.11 enzymatic activity is decreased up to 97% in vitro and TPMT.11 exhibied decreased affinity for 6-MP, 6-TG, and SAM relative to TPMT.1 (16220112, 18708949). Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*11-defining SNV, c.395G>A (p.C132Y), impairs protein function.

CYP2C19*6 was first described in a subject demonstrating decreased mephenytoin metabolism based on urine metabolite concentrations. This correlated with an in vitro study which found CYP2C19*6 had nondetectable enzyme activity with mephenytoin and tolbutamide substrates (9732415). Two additional in vitro studies using different cell systems found CYP2C19*6 had nondetectable enzyme activity with mephenytoin, omeprazole, and clopidogrel substrates (21325430, 25001882). name: Gaohe
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

CYP2C9*2 is assigned decreased function based on definitive evidence in homozygous and heterozygous patients and in vitro experimental data. CYP2C9*2 has been studied for more than 20 years and its association with impaired function compared to wildtype is well-established (29283396, 20150829, 15637526). The phenotype for patients homozygous for the CYP2C9*2 variant was updated from poor metabolizer to intermediate metabolizer after reevaluation of prior and new evidence demonstrating CYP2C9*2 has more enzyme activity than CYP2C9*3, which results in in a similar phenotype for CYP2C9*1/*3 and CYP2C9 *2/*2 patients (12742136, 10413320, 27179628, 25775139, 19082874, 30088221), both of which can be distinguished from those homozygous for CYP2C9*3/*3. Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on definitive evidence.

name: *28x2
findings: CYP2D6*28x2 was deposit into PharmVar. CYP2D6*28 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*28 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from nondetectable to similar activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function for CYP2D6*28. CYP2D6*28x2 is also assigned uncertain function as it cannot yet be determined what the phenotype of a duplicated allele with uncertain function would be clinically similar to.

NAT2*49 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*49 is characterized by the core SNVs c.341T>C (p.I114T) and c.578C>T (p.T193M). Published in silico modeling predicts that c.578C>T (p.T193M) impairs protein function based on exposure of hydrophobic residues that may facilitate protein aggregation or targeted degradation (19909761). Similarly, six out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.578C>T (p.T193M) impairs protein function. c.341T>C (p.I114T) defines NAT2*5 which has repeatedly shown decreased N-acetyltransferase activity in numerous in vivo and in vitro studies. Additionally, an in vitro study showed that NAT2*5 is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*49 would presumably be degraded in the same manner. Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C (p.I114T) impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*49 is assigned decreased function based on c.341T>C. Despite this, it remains unknown whether there are any combinatorial effects associated with c.341T>C and c.578C>T

CYP2D6*41 is assigned decreased function based on strong evidence in heterozygous and homozygous subjects and in vitro studies. Subjects carrying CYP2D6*41 have demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype and CYP2D6*2 subjects with various substrates (11037799, 15289790, 17112815, 19364831). In vitro studies found CYP2D6*41 has decreased protein expression compared to wildtype (17001295, 19364831). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.25 based on strong evidence.

NUDT15*15 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. An in vitro study expressed NUDT15*15 in HEK293 cells and found that NUDT15*15 protein levels were comparable to NUDT15*1 and NUDT15*15 had no effect on thiopurine sensitivity in cells (32094176). Five out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.467T>A (p.L156Q) impairs protein function.

CYP2D6*81 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*81 was identified in a subject demonstrating impaired enzyme activity based on experiencing adverse effects with venlafaxine and subsequently elevated nortriptyline levels (23799451). CYP2D6*81 is defined by an early stop codon yielding a truncated and nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

CYP2D6*7 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*7 was first described in subjects demonstrating decreased sparteine metabolism (7845481). Two in vitro studies found CYP2D6*7 had nondetectable or markedly decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with tamoxifen, bufuralol, dextromethorphan, and primaquine substrates (24647041, 30366777). CYP2D6*7 is defined by an amino substitution near the active site disrupting protein folding and resulting in a nonfunctional enzyme based on computational analysis (7845481). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

CYP2D6*45 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. Subjects with CYP2D6 *5/*45 genotype demonstrated similar dextromethorphan metabolism compared to wildtype subjects (15768052). In vitro studies found CYP2D6*45 demonstrated similar activity compared to wildtype with dextromethorphan and bufuralol substrates (15768052, 24647041). Although one in vitro study found CYP2D6*45 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype with a primaquine substrate (30366777) the experts did not find this result convincing as CYP2D6*45 demonstrated similar activity compared to wildtype towards a majority of substrates as well as in vivo. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function with an activity value of 1 based on limited evidence.

clinicalfunctionalstatus: increased risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR1 1494C>T is assigned an increased risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on strong evidence. Screening of individuals or probands of individuals with aminoglycoside-hearing looss identified 1494C>T in a number of cases (14681830, 16380089, 17085680, 17434445, 17698030, 17698299, 18830133, 19682603, 20100600, 21205314, 27397648, 29876232). LCLs and/or cybrind cell lines with 1494C>T mutation have reduced growth in the presence of aminoglycosides (14681830, 15722487). Paromomycin shows increased binding to rRNA constructs carrying the T allele (19687236). Increased Kd for neomycin binding to A-site constructs carrying the T allele compared to those carrying the C allele (28103015). Cybrid lines with the 1494C>T mutation grown in gentamicin have reduced ATP production and altered mitochondria morphology (25474306). Therefore, consensus among experts was this variant is associated with increased risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on strong evidence.

NAT2*75 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*75 is defined by the core SNVs c.264G>A (p.M88I) and c.341T>C (p.I114T). c.341T>C (p.I114T) defines NAT2*5 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. In vitro studies also show that NAT2*5 (which contains the core SNV c.341T>C, p.I114T) is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*75 would presumably be degraded in the same manner. Additionally, five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C (p.I114T) impairs protein function. There is no literature describing function for c.264G>A. Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.264G>A (p.M88I) is neutral for protein function. NAT2*75 is assigned decreased function based on c.341T>C. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.341T>C and c.264G>A are uncertain.

NAT2*52 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*52 is characterized by the core SNVs c.518A>G (p.K173R), c.590G>A (p.R197Q) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no experimental evidence describing function for c.518A>G (p.K173R). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.518A>G (p.K173R) is neutral for protein function. Similarly, published in silico analysis indicates that c.518A>G does not alter protein function (29273096). c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*52 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. While c.518A>G is not predicted to impair protein function, the combinatorial effects associated with c.590G>A and c.518A>G are uncertain.

CYP2D6*8 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*8 was first described in a subject demonstrating decreased debrisoquine and sparteine metabolism (8530011). CYP2D6*8 is defined by an amino substitution resulting in a premature stop codon yielding a truncated and nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

SLCO1B1*41 is assigned no function based on strong evidence. SLCO1B1*41 is defined by c.481+1G>T which disrupts a canonical splice site. An individual genotyped SLCO1B1*31/*41 exhibited an elevated plasma concentration of coproporphyrin I, at a level comparable to that in SLCO1B1*15/*15 patients (39511784). Individuals compound heterozygous for *41 experienced statin-induced rhabdomyolysis (34887219, 39511784). SLCO1B1*41 was significantly associated with altered hexadecanedioate and SLCO1B1-associated oxylipins levels in individuals with *41 vs those without (27602404, 39644095). A large clinical study demonstrated that individuals with SLCO1B1*41 exhibited significantly increased bilirubin levels relative to those without, however there was no significant association with statin-induced myotoxicity (40047317). Additionally, SLCO1B1 loss-of-function has been implicated in the pathogenesis of Rotor syndrome and SLCO1B1*41 has been identified in affected families (22232210). The variant effect predictors CADD and PhD-SNPg predict that c.481+1G>T impairs protein function, which is supported by a published in silico analysis (35631530). Expression of a truncated protein due to aberrant splicing is also supported by SpliceAI predictions.

TPMT*2 is assigned no function based on definitive evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals heterozygous for TPMT*2 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity, while erythrocytes from individuals homozygous for TPMT*2 or heterozygous for TPMT*2 in combination with another no function allele exhibited low enzymatic activity (7862671, 9246020, 9695718, 15226673, 23252716, 31005613, 12880540, 14508387, 14656901, 9103127, 11372592, 18021342, 9831928, 14985890). Patients carrying TPMT*2 alleles exhibited increased plasma concentrations of TGNs, decreased plasma concentrations of methylated metabolites, and tolerated lower doses of thiopurines (19057372, 18021342, 10580024). In vitro studies found that TPMT.2 protein expression and enzymatic activity was decreased by 75-100% relative to TPMT*1 in yeast (7862671, 9177237) and mammalian cells (16220112, 9177237, 18708949), as well as patient erythrocytes (9177237, 9695718). TPMT.2 decreased protein expression has been attributed to rapid degradation by the proteasome (9177237, 16220112). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*2-defining SNV, c.238G>C (p.A80P), impairs protein function.

CYP2B6*43 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R; c.1390G>A, p.A464T) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*43 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*43 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information). As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). A single in vitro study reported decreased function for p.A464T using buproprion and efavirez as probes (38596098). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

name: Taipei, Chinese-3
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

NAT2*14 is assigned decreased function based on definitive evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*14 allele as "slow" . Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". ". NAT2*14 is characterized by the core SNVs c.191G>A (p.R64Q) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly exhibited increased function in vivo and in vitro. The functional SNV c.191G>A (p.R64Q) has been studied for more than 25 years and has shown decreased NAT2 N-acetylation activity in both clinical and in vitro studies using various substrates. Clinical studies demonstrate that individuals homozygous for NAT2*14 or heterozygous for NAT2*14 in combination with another decreased function allele exhibit decreased acetylation activity toward caffeine metabolites (8102597, 7668286, 9110365, 9565293, 21750470, 24221535, 28653770), isoniazid (9154882, 17505821), sulfamethazine (21946899), and dapsone (10762005). Six out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.191G>A (p.R64Q) impairs protein function. Published in silico modeling predicts that c.191G>A decreases protein stability and hinders electrostatic interactions (18680467). In vitro studies have demonstrated that NAT2*14 exhibits decreased protein expression and thermostability compared to the former reference, NAT2*4 (11337936, 17434923, 8081359).

CYP2B6*27 (c.593T>C, p.M198T) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*27 is assigned uncertain function. Three in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found that CYP2B6*27 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference for most substrates, although increased activity was seen for one non-drug probe substrate (21746968, 17235330, 30201214). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. One subject with the CYP2B6*6/*27 genotype had an elevated efavirenz AUC compared to the *1 reference (17235330). Consensus was not achieved among experts due to insufficient evidence of this variant in human subjects. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned based on inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*30 (CYP2B7-CYP2B6 hybrid gene) – No function: CYP2B6*30 is assigned no function. This allele has a hybrid gene consisting of CYP2B6 (exons 1-4) and CYP2B7 (exons 5-9) harboring 15 amino acid changes compared to the protein encoded by the CYP2B6*1 reference. Among those is a premature stop codon (p.R378X) which is also found in CYP2B6*28 (23164804). This allele has been found in a duplication structure; it remains uncertain, however, whether the other gene in the duplication is a *1, *4 or *6 (or potentially other). Although CYP2B6*30 has no function, the function of this duplication allele depends on the nature of the other gene copy. One in vitro study found that primary human hepatocytes of subjects with a CYP2B6*30 had significantly lower mRNA and protein expression and enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (28181240). The same study also showed that the AUC for 4-OH-cyclophosphamide was significantly lower for patients with *1/*30 compared to *1/*1 (28181240). In this study, the presence of a CYP2B6*30 was inferred by subjects having three gene copies and sequence analysis of the *30; however, the study did not provide any information regarding the nature of the second gene copy of the duplication. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*6 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect and c.785A>G, p.K262R) - Decreased function: CYP2B6*6 is assigned decreased function based on in vivo (homozygous and heterozygous patients) and in vitro studies. Significantly lower protein expression and activity was found in human liver microsomes, and increased plasma exposure of CYP2B6 substrates was found in healthy volunteers and patients. In a panel of 87 human liver microsome samples, the CYP2B6*6 allele was associated with significantly decreased protein expression and activity, measured by bupropion 4-hydroxylation and efavirenz 8-hydroxylation (17559344). The mechanism was predominantly aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T (but not c.785A>G) leading to reduced content of functional mRNA, protein, and activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). Multiple in vitro studies using human liver microsomes (17559344, 22232427, (23550066) and cDNA expression systems (21659470, 32238417, 35538637) found that CYP2B6*6 has decreased enzyme activity and metabolism of efavirenz, bupropion, methadone and ketamine. Although two other in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found increased activity (12642465, 30201214), the experts did not find these results convincing. This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. Multiple studies evaluating efavirenz plasma concentrations among patients with HIV found that CYP2B6*6 carriers had higher efavirenz concentrations compared to the *1 reference (e.g., 15194512, 17235330, 27799204). A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among HIV patients taking efavirenz for more than three months similarly found that CYP2B6*6 was associated with greater long term efavirenz exposure (26655325). Other studies using c.516G>T as a tagSNP for CYP2B6*6 (15622315) showed that G/T and T/T genotypes (compared to G/G) were associated with significantly higher efavirenz plasma exposure during HIV therapy. Several studies found that plasma exposure of the active bupropion metabolite hydroxybupropion was less in CYP2B6*6 carriers than in the *1 reference, although bupropion concentration and clearance were unchanged (23344581, 25565674, 29756345, 34752647). Multiple clinical studies of methadone, evaluating metabolism and/or clearance, have shown that methadone plasma exposure (S- more than R-) is greater and/or metabolism and clearance is less in CYP2B6*6 carriers compared to the *1 reference (16338275, 17178267, 17329992, 25456329, 26389554, 28723731, 32302325 ). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on strong evidence.

CYP2C19*30 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C19*30 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, omeprazole, voriconazole, and nebivolol substrates (26153442, 30464555, 29098786). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2C19*35 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2C19*35 is defined by a splicing defect resulting in a truncated protein (26021325). This splicing defect is also found in CYP2C19*2 which definitively has no function. Although subjects with CYP2C19 *1/*35 did not demonstrate significantly decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype based on urine metabolites after mephenytoin 100mg once (26021325), the experts did not find this result convincing due to the small sample size. CYP2C19*17 is assigned increased function based on strong evidence in homozygous and heterozygous subjects. Subjects with CYP2C19 *1/*17 and CYP2C19 *17/*17 genotype demonstrated increased omeprazole metabolism compared to wildtype subjects based on plasma metabolite measurements. The predicted AUC of omeprazole in CYP2C19*17/*17 patients was 63% of the predicted AUC in wildtype subjects. Additionally, in a separate population, CYP2C19*17/*17 subjects demonstrated increased mephenytoin metabolism compared to wildtype and CYP2C19 *1/*17 subjects. A murine study found increased gene transcription of CYP2C19*17 likely results in the observed increased enzyme activity (16413245). CYP2C19 *17/*17 patients taking escitalopram had 42% serum escitalopram concentrations compared to wildtype (17625515). Also, in adolescents with generalized anxiety disorder, patients with *4/*17 or *17/*17 had lower AUC/CMAX compared to normal (*1/*1) and poor metabolizers and expected differences in the ratios of s-citalopram to its metabolite, desmethylcitalopram (PMID 32857933). Additionally, CYP2C19 *1/*17 and *17/*17 carriers in naturalistically studied depressed adults had 10% and 20% lower serum concentration compared to CYP2C19 NMs (29325448). In healthy adults, *17/*17 was associated with lower sertraline AUC vs normal metabolizers (29136336), although in a separate study involving more than 1200 patients, the CYP2C19*17 allele was associated with 8% lower serum concentration compared to NMs (31649299). CYP2C19 *17/*17 patients taking clopidogrel had lower ADP induced platelet aggregation likely as a result of increased clopidogrel metabolism to its active metabolite compared to wildtype and CYP2C19 *1/*17 patients (20083681). Therefore, consensus among experts was increased function based on strong evidence.

CYP2C9*17 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence due to conflicting in vitro data and no clinical data. In an in vitro study CYP2C9*17 demonstrated 32% warfarin clearance compared to wildtype, however, similar tolbutamide clearance compared to wildtype was demonstrated in the same study as well as a separate study (23752738, 16099926). Therefore, consensus among experts was not achieved and uncertain function was assigned.
CYP2D6*85 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

NAT2*47 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. NAT2*47 is characterized by the core SNVs c.191G>A (p.R64Q), c.683C>T (p.P228L), and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. Published in silico modeling predicts that c.683C>T (p.P228L) impairs protein function based on the exposure of hydrophobic residues that may facilitate protein aggregation and/or targeted protein degradation (19909761). However, two out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.683C>T (p.P228L) impairs protein function. c.191G>A (p.R64Q, defining NAT2*14) has repeatedly shown decreased NAT2 function in numerous in vivo and in vitro studies. Six out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.191G>A (p.R64Q) impairs protein function. Published in silico modeling predicts that c.191G>A (p.R64Q) decreases protein stability and hinders electrostatic interactions (18680467). A NAT2 phenotyping study identified one patient with the genotype NAT2*5/*47 and this individual was classified a "slow acetylator" (24533708). In this study, slow acetylators exhibited lower metabolic ratios of acetyl-INH/INH that were statistically significant compared to both rapid and intermediate acetylators, as well as an increased INH half-life, increased INH AUC values, and decreased INH CL/F values (24533708). NAT2*47 is assigned decreased function based on the function of c.191G>A (p.R64Q). Despite this, it remains unknown whether there are any combinatorial effects associated with c.683C>T and c.191G>A

name: c.5132A>G
findings: PS4_Sup, PP1_Sup, PS3_Mod

CYP2D6*4 is assigned no function based on strong evidence. CYP2D6*4 has consistently been described in subjects demonstrating decreased metabolism of various CYP2D6 substrates (2211621, 11266079, 1978251, 1978565). Two in vitro studies found CYP2D6*4 had undetectable protein expression (2211621, 11266079). CYP2D6*4 is defined by a splicing defect resulting in a nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on strong evidence.

CYP2D6*113 is assigned uncertain function. CYP2D6*113 is defined by a SNP which in silico studies predict to be a deleterious amino acid change (31368850). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

name: Acrokorinthos

name: Asahi
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

TPMT*13 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. TPMT*13 was identified in an individual who experienced severe leucopenia after standard azathioprine dosing. In vitro studies found that TPMT.13 protein expression is decreased, enzymatic activity is decreased by 40-80%, and TPMT.13 exhibited decreased affinity for 6-MP, 6-TG, and SAM relative to TPMT.1 in mammalian (16220112, 18708949) and yeast cells (13679074). Six out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*13-defining SNV, c.83A>T (p.E28V), impairs protein function.

NAT2*53 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*53 is characterized by the core SNVs c.248G>C (p.G83A), c.590G>A (p.R197Q) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no experimental evidence describing function for c.248G>C (p.G83A). Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.248G>C (p.G83A) impairs protein function. Similarly, published in silico analysis indicates that c.248G>C (p.G83A) is deleterious for protein function (Abodlaa et al. 2019; Bilal et al. 2022). c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*53 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.248G>C and c.590G>A are uncertain.

CYP2C9*22 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In one in vitro study, CYP2C9*22 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with a warfarin and tolbutamide substrate (23752738). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to insufficient evidence.

CYP2C9*45 is assigned no function based on limited evidence in one heterozygous patient and in vitro studies. One subject with CYP2C9*1/*45 genotype exhibited nearly twice the AUC and half the oral clearance compared to wildtype subjects based on tolbutamide plasma concentrations after one 500mg dose (25614704). Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*45 demonstrated markedly decreased clearance compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25144335, 25951663, 25476996, 25832633, 25924705, 27163851, 23844998, 25614704). Although two in vitro studies showed CYP2C9*45 had similar or increased activity compared to wildtype, the experts did not find these results convincing as in these studies CYP2C9*3 which definitively has no function, demonstrated increased or similar activity compared to wildtype in these studies (25142737, 25884291). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function or decreased function alleles.

CYP2D6*26 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*26 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from increased to decreased activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (18838503, 24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function.

name: m.988G>A
clinicalfunctionalstatus: uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR 988G>A is assigned an uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on inadequate evidence. Screening of individuals with aminoglycoside-hearing loss identified this variant in one study (18830133). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss due to insufficient evidence.

CYP2C9*24 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence in a heterozygous patient and in vitro studies. A patient on a stable dose of warfarin with CYP2C9 *2/*24 genotype had the lowest daily warfarin dose requirement and warfarin clearance based on plasma concentrations compared to patients with CYP2C9 *1/*2 genotype and matched for age, warfarin indication and co-administrated medications (16543980). An in vitro study similarly found CYP2C9*24 exhibited no enzyme activity with warfarin or tolbutamide substrates (23752738). No CYP2C9*24 protein was detected in a separate in vitro study suggesting the enzyme may be improperly folded (17332144). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

CYP2C9*38 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*38 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype for multiple substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25144335, 25951663, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25924705, 23844998). Although two in vitro studies showed CYP2C9*38 had similar activity compared to wildtype, the experts did not find these results convincing as in these studies CYP2C9*2 which definitively has decreased function, demonstrated increased activity and similar activity compared to wildtype in these studies (25142737, 27163851). Additionally, the experts did not deem an in vitro study where CYP2C9*38 demonstrated activity ranging from decreased to increased compared to wildtype with 7-ethoxycoumarin, flavanone, and steroid substrates (28758225) as convincing evidence because it could not be ruled out that these results which differed from the majority of in vitro studies were due to the specific system used in the study. Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2C9*63 is assigned uncertain funciton due to inadequate evidence. The nonsynonymous variants present in CYP2C9*63 is predicted to be deleterious in both SIFT and Polyphen predictions. There are no in vitro or clinical studies reported. Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

name: Crispim
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

CYP2C9*29 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*29 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype (23752738, 23844998, 24118918, 25951663, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25924705, 24077631). Although two in vitro studies showed CYP2C9*29 had similar activity compared to wildtype, the experts did not find these results convincing as in these studies CYP2C9*2 which definitively has decreased function, demonstrated increased or similar activity compared to wildtype in these studies (25142737, 27163851). Other studies, however, also showed CYP2C9*29 has similar clearance compared to wildtype (16788382, 25614704). Although the experts recognized there is conflicting evidence, the experts did not find it convincing as it was determined the activity of CYP2C9*29 toward most substrates was similar to CYP2C9*2 which definitively has decreased function. Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5.

CYP2B6*38 contains the variants which define CYP2B6*6 in addition to a third variant which causes a premature stop codon. CYP2B6*38 was identified in a patient taking efavirenz who developed neurologic symptoms and was found to have an efavirenz plasma concentration 40-fold above normal limit. Efavirenz was discontinued and efavirenz plasma concentrations returned to normal after 10 weeks in addition to resolution of neurologic symptoms (23640958). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence.

CYP2C9*10 is assigned uncertain function based on conflicting in vitro data and no clinical data. Although CYP2C9*10 demonstrated decreased warfarin clearance (23752738) it also demonstrated similar activity with a tolbutamide substrate (15284535) and activity ranging from decreased to normal with 7-ethoxycoumarin, flavanone, and steroid substrates (28758225) compared to wildtype. Therefore, consensus among experts was not achieved and uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2C9*13 is assigned no function based on definitive evidence in heterozygous patients. Subjects with CYP2C9*1/*13 genotype repeatedly demonstrated decreased clearance and increased AUC compared to wildtype subjects and similar clearance and AUC compared to CYP2C9*1/*3 subjects in multiple studies with different substrates (19604036, 21395648, 21726410, 21842338, 22735459, 27377818, 27864660, 15606435). Two studies correlated in vitro data with clinical data and also found increased AUC and decreased clearance compared to wildtype with lornoxicam and tolbutamide (15764711, 25614704). Additionally, a CYP2C9*3/*13 subject had a greater decrease in tolbutamide clearance compared to the CYP2C9*1/*13 subject (25614704). This was also demonstrated in another CYP2C9*3/*13 subject with decreased lornoxicam clearance and increased AUC compared to wildtype, CYP2C9 *1/*3, and CYP2C9*1/*2 subjects (15606435). Multiple in vitro studies demonstrate CYP2C9*13 exhibits decreased metabolism comparable to that observed for CYP2C9*3 for numerous substrates (23844998, 24118918, 24464553, 25142737, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 27163851, 24077631, 25951663, 19541829, 20814155, 23752738, 16308280, 25144335) and one model suggests the decreased metabolism across substrates is due to a side chain rotation which blocks the catalytic site (16740353). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on definitive evidence.

CYP2D6*96 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*96 had nondetectable enzyme with various substrates (25469868, 26310775, 26666748, 26544071, 26406933, 27109434, 27097346, 27016952, 26542054, 26937172, 26968424, 27203132, 27251229, 27354764, 27538917). CYP2D6*96 is defined by an early stop codon yielding a truncated nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

CYP2D6*161 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*161 is defined by the two SNPs defining the normal function *2 allele and two SNP that are only found on this star allele of which one, 2591_2592insT causes a premature stop codon (E273X) that likely renders the allele nonfunctional.

Although two in vitro studies showed CYP2C9*37 had similar activity compared to wildtype, the experts did not find these results convincing as in these studies CYP2C9*3 which definitively has no function, demonstrated increased activity compared to wildtype in these studies (25142737, 25884291). An additional in vitro study showed CYP2C9*37 had similar activity compared to wildtype (25144335), however, the experts did not find this result convincing as the function of CYP2C9*37 demonstrated decreased function toward the majority of substrates.
