CYP2D6*11 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*11 is defined by a splicing defect yielding a nonfunctional enzyme and was identified in a subject demonstrating markedly decreased sparteine metabolism (8563771, 9241659). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

CYP2D6*43 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*43 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from nondetectable to similar activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function.

CYP2D6*110 is assigned uncertain function. CYP2D6*110 is defined by a SNP which in silico studies predict to be a deleterious amino acid change (31368850). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

findings: PS4_Mod, PM1, PP3_Mod

CYP2C19*23 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C19*23 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from decreased to increased compared to wildtype with various substrates (25001882, 27578295, 30464555, 31713493). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

CYP2C9*32 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In one in vitro study, CYP2C9*32 demonstrated similar enzyme activity compared to wildtype with a warfarin and tolbutamide substrate (23752738). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to insufficient evidence.

CYP2D6*162 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. CYP2D6*162 is defined by the two SNPs defining the normal function *2 allele and the SNP defining the normal function *27 allele.

TPMT*27 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*27 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity and the patient received a reduced dose of azathioprine (19945438). An in vitro study using a mammalian cell expression system found that TPMT.27 exhibited decreased protein expression that is attributed to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome, as well as clearance by autophagy (19945438). TPMT.27 expressed in mammalian cells exhibited 90% decreased enzymatic activity compared to TPMT.1 (19945438). Molecular dynamics simulations suggest that TPMT.27 exhibited decreased protein stability (19945438). In line with these results, nine out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*27-defining SNV, c.319T>G (p.Y107D), impairs protein function.

name: c.1057C>T

name: *17x2
findings: CYP2D6*17x2 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*17 is assigned decreased function based on subjects demonstrating decreased enzyme activity with various substrates (8764380, 8971426, 11823760). Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function with an activity value of 1 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing a duplication of this allele would demonstrate greater enzyme activity than that of the nonduplicated allele and be clinically similar to phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2D6*53 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*53 had similar enzyme activity compared to wildtype with multiple substrates (18784265, 24647041). Although one in vitro study found CYP2D6*53 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype with a primaquine substrate (30366777) the experts did not find this result convincing as CYP2D6*53 demonstrated similar activity compared to wildtype towards a majority of substrates. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function with an activity value of 1 based on limited evidence.
CYP2D6*140 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

CYP2D6*91 is assigned decreased function with an activity value of 0.25 due to limited evidence. *91 contains 2989G>A splicing SNP found in *41 (decreased function). In vitro studies found CYP2D6*91 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from similar to decreased activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (25469868, 26310775, 26666748, 26544071, 26406933, 27109434, 27097346, 27016952, 26542054, 26937172, 26968424, 27203132, 27251229, 27354764, 27538917): however, in the cases of normal activity, the effect of the 2989G>A splice SNP found in *41 was not captured in the in vitro system utilized which may explain activity comparable to *1 or *2. Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on limited evidence.
CYP2D6*118 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

name: c.4775C>T
findings: PP3_Mod

CYP2B6*37 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.548T>G, p.V183G; c.785A>G, p.K262R) – Decreased function: CYB2B6*37 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*37 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information) (23418033). Multiple in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found that CYP2B6*37 has no enzyme activity with bupropion and efavirenz compared to the *1 reference (23418033, 30201214). However, there was no in vivo data or data for liver microsomes. As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2D6*135 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate literature describing function of this allele. One in vitro study demonstrated decreased intrinsic clearance CLint (Vmax/Km) of 32.3% and 45.8% compared to *1 for tamoxifen N-desmethylation and dextromethorphan O-demethylation, respectively (32759992). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

CYP2D6*34 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*34 is defined by a common SNP found in more than 50 different CYP2D6 alleles including CYP2D6*2 which is assigned normal function. An in vitro study found CYP2D6*34 had similar enzyme activity compared to wildtype with dextromethorphan and bufuralol substrates (24647041). Although CYP2D6*34 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity with other substrates (24647041, 30366777), the experts did not find this result convincing that the magnitude of decreased activity was clinically significant. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function with an activity value of 1 based on limited evidence.

name: Chinese-5
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

SLCO1B1*25 is assigned uncertain function based on no in vitro evidence and limited clinical data. One clinical study showed no statistical difference in methotrexate clearance for one heterozygous patient (22147369). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

NAT2*51 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. There is no experimental evidence describing function for c.121A>T (p.N41Y). Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.121A>T impairs protein function which agrees with published computational predictions indicating reduced function for c.121A>T (29273096).
CYP2D6*115 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

CYP2D6*146 is assigned uncertain function. CYP2D6*146 is defined by three SNPs all of which have been found in other star alleles; none of these SNPs have been implicated to cause no function; two are the defining SNPs of the normal function *2 allele and one is the defining SNP of uncertain function *43 allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function.

name: *27x2
findings: CYP2D6*27x2 was deposited into PharmVar. CYP2D6*27 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*27 demonstrated similar activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (18784265, 18838503). Although some in vitro studies found CYP2D6*27 demonstrated modestly decreased activity it was not convincing the magnitude of decreased activity was clinically significant (24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function with an activity value of 1 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically similar to phenotypes comprising normal function alleles. Based on other normal function alleles such as *1, *2, *45, a duplication of a normal function allele is assigned an activity value of 2 and increased function.

name: *35x2
findings: CYP2D6*35x2 is assigned increased function based on limited evidence in heterozygous subjects. CYP2D6*35x2 was described in a subject with CYP2D6*1/*35x2 genotype demonstrating increased enzyme activity with the greatest sparteine metabolism among 195 subjects based on urine metabolites. Additionally, a subject with CYP2D6 *5/*35x2 genotype demonstrated similar sparteine metabolism compared to wildtype subjects indicating increased enzyme activity (9511177). Therefore, consensus among experts was increased function with an activity value of 2 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2D6*36 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence in heterozygous subjects and in vitro studies. CYP2D6*36 has been described in subjects with CYP2D6 *1/*36 and CYP2D6 *2/*36 genotype demonstrating decreased metabolism and subjects with CYP2D6 *4/*36 demonstrating poor metabolism with debrisoquine and dextromethorphan substrates (9918137, 16415111). Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*36 had nondetectable or markedly decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (7935325, 18784265, 24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on moderate evidence.

name: *36x2
findings: CYP2D6*36x2 is assigned no function based on limited evidence in a heterozygous subject. CYP2D6*36x2 has been described in a subject with CYP2D6*21/*36x2 genotype demonstrating poor metabolism with a debrisoquine substrate (12439227). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing a duplication of this allele would be clinically similar to phenotypes comprising the nonduplicated allele.
CYP2D6*108 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

name: c.934C>T
findings: 5-fluorouracil (in vitro)

NAT2*60 is assigned unknown function based on a lack of evidence. NAT2*60 is characterized by the core SNVs c.491T>C (p.I164T) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.491T>C (p.I164T). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.491T>C (p.I164T) is neutral for protein function

name: *10x2
findings: CYP2D6*10x2 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Three subjects with CYP2D6*10/*10x2 genotype demonstrated similar dextromethorphan metabolism compared to CYP2D6*10/*10 subjects and decreased metabolism compared to CYP2D6 *1/*1, CYP2D6 *1/*2, and CYP2D6*1/*10 subjects based on urine metabolites (15149890). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles and clinically similar to phenotypes comprising decreased function alleles.

CYP2B6*46 (c.740A>C, p.H247P) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*46 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence describing function. A single in vitro study reported decreased function for p.H247P using buproprion and efavirez as probes (38596098).

name: *41x2
findings: CYP2D6*41x2 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*41 is assigned decreased function based on decreased protein expression and enzyme activity (11037799, 15289790, 17112815, 19364831, 17001295). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing a duplication of this allele would demonstrate greater protein expression and enzyme activity than that of the nonduplicated allele and be clinically similar to phenotypes comprising decreased function alleles.

CYP2D6*42 is assigned no function based on limited evidence in a heterozygous subject. CYP2D6*42 has been described in subjects with CYP2D6 *17/*42 genotype demonstrating decreased metabolism and a subject with CYP2D6 *40/*42 genotype demonstrating poor metabolism with a dextromethorphan substrate (12811367). CYP2D6*42 is defined by a base pair insertion resulting in a frameshift yielding a truncated and nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

TPMT*12 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. Patients genotyped TPMT*1/*12 exhibited TPMT enzymatic activity that is comparable to TPMT*1/*1 patients and there was no difference in 6-MP dose intensity between TPMT*1/*12 and TPMT*1/*1 patients (27564568). In vitro studies using mammalian cell expression systems found that TPMT.12 protein expression is decreased, enzymatic activity is decreased >50%, and TPMT.12 exhibited decreased affinity for 6-TG and SAM compared to TPMT.1 (16220112, 18708949). TPMT.12 also exhibited an increased rate of protein degradation compared to TPMT.1 (16220112). TPMT.12 enzymatic activity is decreased by 75% compared to TPMT.1 in yeast cells (13679074). An additional in vitro study found that TPMT.12 protein abundance in mammalian cells was comparable to TPMT.1 (29785012). Four out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*12-defining SNV, c.374C>T (p.S125L), impairs protein function.

functioncomments: UGT1A1*80 is in very high linkage disequilibrium with *28 and *37. However, the function of the *80 allele alone, in the absence of *28 or *37, is uncertain. If a patient is only tested for *80, decreased function may be inferred.

findings: CYP2D6*5 is assigned no function based on definitive evidence. CYP2D6*5 has been studied for more than 20 years and its association with impaired function is well-established (1673290, 31544239). CYP2D6*5 is defined by a deletion of the CYP2D6 gene. CYP2D6*5 was first identified in a homozygous subject demonstrating decreased sparteine metabolism (1673290). An additional study found liver samples of seven subjects heterozygous for CYP2D6*5 demonstrated decreased intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with a bufuralol substrate (11266079). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on definitive evidence.

CYP2D6*10 is assigned decreased function based on strong evidence. CYP2D6*10 has consistently been described in subjects demonstrating decreased metabolism of various CYP2D6 substrates, with metabolic ratios greater than those observed in wildtype subjects but less than poor metabolizers (8764380, 8287064, 19364831). In vitro studies using different cell systems similarly found CYP2D6*10 demonstrates decreased intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with various substrates (27538917, 27354764, 27251229, 27203132, 26968424, 26937172, 26652007, 26542054, 26310775, 25469868, 30366777, 24647041, 11266079, 18784265). As part of the standardization of CYP2D6 genotype to phenotype translation among CPIC and the Dutch Pharmacogenetics Working Group, CYP2D6*10 was reevaluated as the evidence suggests the activity of the CYP2D6*10 is considerably lower compared to other decreased function alleles and the activity value of 0.5 (previously assigned) may overestimate metabolic capacity of patients with this allele (31647186). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.25 based on strong evidence.

CYP2D6*23 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*23 demonstrated modest decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with tamoxifen, bufuralol, dextromethorphan, and primaquine substrates (24647041, 30366777). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse and it was not convincing the magnitude of decreased activity was clinically significant. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2D6*109 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. *109 has the 2616delAAG (also present in *9 with decreased function-PMID 1844820; 8101460; 11266079; 24647041; 30366777) and 3184G>A (V338M) which is a core SNP of *29. One study compared activity of V338M vs that of V136I (both present in *29) and found that both decrease activity in vitro (PMID 11470994). Because both variants have been shown to have decreased function in vitro, consensus of the experts was decreased function with limited evidence.

CYP2D6*119 is assigned decreased function wiht an activity value of 0.25 due to limited evidence describing function of this allele. *119 contains the splice SNP 2989G>A that is also found in *41 (decreased function; 11037799; 15289790; 17001295; 17112815; 19364831). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function due to limited evidence.

NAT2*57 is assigned unknown function based on a lack of evidence. NAT2*57 is characterized by the core SNVs c.321C>A (p.H107Q) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro Eight out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.321C>A (p.H107Q) impairs protein function

name: *4x≥3
findings: CYP2D6*4x≥3 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. CYP2D6*4x3 was detected in two samples (266029920). CYP2D6*4 duplication has been described in subjects demonstrating decreased metabolism of dextromethorphan or debrisoquine (10022755, 12152006). CYP2D6*4 is defined by a splicing defect resulting in a nonfunctional protein and gene duplication or multiplications would therefore not impact enzyme activity. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on moderate evidence.

name: c.3527C>T
findings: BP4

CYP2C19*14 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. CYP2C19*14 demonstrated similar enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, omeprazole, and other substrates (21325430, 21692664). In a separate in vitro study CYP2C19*14 demonstrated decreased activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin and clopidogrel substrates (25001882). Therefore, consensus among experts was not achieved and uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2C19*36 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2C19*36 is a complete deletion of the CYP2C19 gene and was identified by interrogation of multiple databases of clinical chromosomal microarray data (31260137). Therefore, because a gene deletion would not result in protein formation, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function and decreased function alleles.

NAT2*43 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. NAT2*43 is characterized by the core SNV c.609G>T (p.E203D). Published in silico modeling predicts that c.609G>T (p.E203D) does not alter NAT2 protein function (19909761). Similarly, Nine out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.609G>T (p.E203D) is neutral/tolerated for NAT2 protein function. The limited clinical data available for NAT2*43 was not sufficient to influence the allele clinical functional status.(24444407).

name: c.7372C>T
findings: PS3_Mod, PS4_Mod, PM1_Sup, PM5, PP3_Mod

CYP2D6*21 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*21 has been described in subjects demonstrating markedly decreased metabolism of debrisoquine and dextromethorphan (10471060, 15618746). CYP2D6*21 is defined by a base insertion resulting in a frameshift and premature stop codon, yielding a truncated and nonfunctional enzyme. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

name: m.930G>A
clinicalfunctionalstatus: uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR1 930G>A is assigned an uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on inadequate evidence. Screening of individuals with aminoglycoside-hearing loss identified this variant in one study (18830133). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss due to insufficient evidence. While PMID 18830133 states that "..., 11 of the 151 sequences demonstrated an A > G transition at base position 930. Two of these patients had moderate-to-severe hearing loss." Table 3 of the article shows the G allele as the Human consensus and the 11 subjects as having the A allele. Furthermore, the guideline authors have reviewed institutional sample data and confirmed that the G allele is the reference allele. Therefore the variant is referenced as m.930G>A as of October 2025.

CYP2D6*133 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate literature describing function of this allele. One in vitro study demonstrated substabtially decreased intrinsic clearance CLint (Vmax/Km) of 2.5% and 4.5% compared to *1 for tamoxifen N-desmethylation and dextromethorphan O-demethylation, respectively. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

CYP2D6*157 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. CYP2D6*157 is defined by the five SNPs defining the decreased function *29 allele and one additional SNP that is not found on any other star allele.

name: Hermoupolis
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

NAT2*16 is assigned decreased function based on definitive evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*16 allele as "slow" . Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". NAT2*16 is characterized by the core SNVs c.341T>C (p.I114T) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly exhibited increased function in vivo and in vitro. The functional SNV c.341T>C (p.I114T) in NAT2*16 has been studied for more than 25 years and has shown decreased NAT2 N-acetylation in numerous clinical studies using caffeine (7668286, 9333101, 9891107, 10744128, 20801937, 24221535), sulfamethazine (21946899), dapsone (10762005), or isoniazid (9321764) as a substrate. Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C impairs protein function. An in vitro study using a bacterial expression system measured the N-acetyltransferase capacity of a NAT2 construct containing the SNVs c.341T>C (p.I114T) and c.481C>T (p.L161=) and found this enzyme exhibited decreased activity compared to the former reference NAT2*4, using either 2-aminofluorene or sulfamethazine as substrates (7669073 ). Another in vitro study using a yeast expression system found that NAT2*16 displayed decreased thermostability relative to the former reference NAT2*4 resulting in decreased N-acetylation activity toward sulfamethazine (10574910). NAT2*16 was also shown to have decreased N-acetyltransferase activity in human hepatocytes (genotyped as NAT2*4/*16 or NAT2*6/*16) using isoniazid as a substrate (28752039). In vitro studies also show that NAT2*5 (which contains the core SNV c.341T>C, p.I114T) is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*16 would presumably be degraded in the same manner.

NAT2*42 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. NAT2*42 is characterized by the core SNV c.622T>C (p.Y208H). Published in silico modeling predicts that c.622T>C (p.Y208H) does not alter NAT2 protein function (19909761). Three out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.622T>C (p.Y208H) impairs protein function. There are no in vitro or in vivo functional studies for NAT2*42

name: m.786G>A
clinicalfunctionalstatus: uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR1 786G>A is assigned an uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on inadequate evidence. Screening of individuals with aminoglycoside-hearing loss identified this variant in one study (23969527). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss due to insufficient evidence.

NAT2*31 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*31 is characterized by the core SNVs c.70T>A (p.L24I) and c.341T>C (p.I114T). Published in silico modeling predicts that c.70T>A (p.L24I) does not impair NAT2 protein function (19909761). However, five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.70T>A (p.L24I) impairs protein function and structure-based predictors suggest that c.70T>A (p.L24I) decreases protein stability which could lead to decreased function. c.341T>C (p.I114T, defining NAT2*5) has repeatedly shown decreased NAT2 N-acetylation activity in numerous in vivo and in vitro studies and five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C (p.I114T) impairs protein function. Additionally, an in vitro study showed that NAT2*5 (which contains the core SNV c.341T>C, p.I114T) is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*31 would presumably be degraded in the same manner and is assigned decreased function based on the function of c.341T>C (p.I114T). Despite this, it remains unknown whether c.70T>A has any combinatorial effects with c.341T>C.

SLCO1B1*10 is assigned uncertain function based on in vitro studies demonstrating reduced uptake of estrone sulfate, but not of estradiol 17beta-d-glucuronide and rifampin (11477075, 12490595) and no clinical data. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

NAT2*7 is assigned decreased function based on definitive evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*7 allele as "slow" and possibly substrate dependent. Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". NAT2*7 is characterized by the core SNVs c.857G>A (p.G286E) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly exhibited increased function in vivo and in vitro. The functional SNV c.857G>A (p.G286E) has been studied for more than 25 years and has shown decreased NAT2 N-acetylation activity in both clinical and in vitro studies using various substrates. Clinical studies have demonstrated that individuals homozygous for NAT2*7 or heterozygous for NAT2*7 in combination with another decreased function allele exhibit decreased acetylation activity toward caffeine metabolites (8102597, 7668286, 9891107, 10744128, 20801937, 22970273, 24221535, 28653770), isoniazid (2376572, 9154882, 9321764, 19336229, 35928262), and sulphonamides (1872889, 15032315, 16570187, 18167504, 21946899). Individuals homozygous for NAT2*7 or heterozygous for NAT2*7 in combination with another decreased function allele exhibit increased hydralazine exposure (24702251, 31257615). In vitro studies have shown that NAT2*7 has decreased N-acetylation activity toward numerous substrates (8081359, 7669073), and NAT2*7 exhibits substrate specificity , exhibiting increased affinity for diaminodiphenyl sulfone (dapsone), sulfamethazine, and sulfapyridine, and decreased affinity for its cofactor, acetyl-CoA as compared to the former reference NAT2*4 (17434923, 33815485, 7669073, 11337936). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.857G>A (p.G286E) does not impair protein function. Published in silico modeling predicts that c.857G>A disrupts the size/shape of the protein's active site (18680467), this result combined with the decreased affinity for acetyl-CoA is likely the molecular mechanism for the decreased acetylation activity observed.

NAT2*50 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*50 is characterized by the core SNVs c.7A>G (p.I3V), c.590G>A (p.R197Q), and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature supporting a function for c.7A>G (p.I3V). One out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predicts that c.7A>G (p.I3V) impairs protein function. c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has repeatedly shown decreased N-acetyltransferase activity in numerous in vivo and in vitro studies. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*50 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. While c.7A>G is not predicted to impair protein function, the combinatorial effects associated with c.590G>A and c.7A>G are uncertain.

name: *1x2
findings: CYP2D6*1x2 is assigned increased function based on limited evidence. Subjects with CYP2D6 gene duplications demonstrated increased enzyme activity with the greatest debrisoquine metabolism among 270 subjects based on urine metabolites (7616439). Additionally, debrisoquine metabolism correlated with the number of functional CYP2D6 genes (7616439, 9012401). Therefore, consensus among experts was increased function with an activity value of 2 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.
CYP2D6*74 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

name: Torun
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

NAT2*66 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*66 is characterized by the core SNVs c.590G>A (p.R197Q), c.682C>T (p.P228S) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.682C>T (p.P228S). One out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict c.682C>T (p.P228S) impairs protein function. c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*66 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.682C>T and c.590G>A are uncertain.

name: c.7018T>C
findings: PS4_Sup, PM1

CYP2C19*34 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. CYP2C19*34 was identified in a patient heterozygous for the CYP2C19*2 allele who experienced adverse effects with venlafaxine and a supratherapeutic nortriptyline level. CYP2C19 enzyme function was not adequately assessed as CYP2D6 is involved in metabolism of antidepressants such as nortriptyline and this patient was a CYP2D6 intermediate metabolizer (23799451). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

CYP2D6*138 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. CYP2D6*138 is defined by a splice defect known to decrease activity and an amino acid substitution which an in vitro study found to increase activity compared to *1 when expressed on its own (31536170). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function.

CYP2B6*25 (c.1454A>T, p.Q485L) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*25 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems showed no significant changes in enzyme activity and/or protein expression compared to the *1 reference (20517174, 21746968, 30201214). However, there were no in vivo data available or data in human liver microsomes. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence.

CYP2D6*99 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*99 has been described in a subject with CYP2D6*5/*99 genotype demonstrating markedly decreased metabolism compared to wildtype with dextromethorphan and venlafaxine substrates (29327975). CYP2D6*99 is defined by the same SNPs as CYP2D6*10 in addition to a SNP resulting in an amino acid substitution which disrupts enzyme activity yielding a nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

NAT2*36 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*36 is characterized by the core SNVs c.403C>G (p.L135V), c.590G>A (p.R197Q), and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.403C>G (p.L135V). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.403C>G is neutral/tolerated for protein function. c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*36 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. While c.403C>G is not predicted to impair protein function, it remains unknown whether there are any combinatorial effects associated with c.590G>A and c.403C>G.

NAT2*40 is assigned decreased function based on definitive evidence. NAT2*40 is characterized by the core SNV c.857G>A (p.G286E, defining NAT2*7) which exhibits decreased N-acetylation activity toward sulfamethazine, decreased thermostability, and decreased protein expression in a mammalian cell lines compared to NAT2*4 (17434923). NAT2*40 also exhibits decreased protein levels when expressed in yeast cells (11337936). A clinical NAT2 phenotyping studying using isoniazid (INH) as a probe drug identified 2 patients carrying NAT2*40 (28959610). These individuals were classified among slow acetylators, who exhibited an acetylation index (defined as the ratio of plasma INH concentration at the 3rd hour, in mg/L + 0.6 divided by the dose in mg/kg) below that of rapid acetylators, which were defined as individuals homozygous for NAT2 increased function alleles *1 or *4 (28959610). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.857G>A (p.G286E) does not impair protein function. However, published in silico modeling indicates that c.857G>A (p.G286E) disrupts the size/shape of the protein's active site (18680467).

CYP2C9*59 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. CYP2C9*59 was first described in a patient requiring a low daily warfarin dose of 1.5mg (25994031). No variant was observed in VKORC1 or CYP4F2 in this patient and a subsequent in silico study showed destabilization of the interaction with the heme group may cause reduction in catalytic activity in this allele. In vitro studies found CYP2C9*59 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity with multiple substrates (25142737, 25994031, 25884291). Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2D6*72 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*72 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (19158312, 24647041, 30366777). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

NAT2*44 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. NAT2*44 is characterized by the core SNV c.403C>G (p.L135V). There is no experimental evidence describing function for c.403C>G (p.L135V). Published in silico modeling predicts that c.403C>G (p.L135V) does not alter NAT2 protein function (19909761). Similarly, ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.403C>G (p.L135V) is neutral/tolerated for NAT2 protein function. The limited clinical data available for NAT2*44 was not sufficient to influence the allele clinical functional status (24444407). Additionally, a NAT2 phenotyping study identified a patient with the genotype NAT2*5/*44, however this individual was excluded from phenotype analysis (28959610).

NAT2*62 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*62 is characterized by the core SNVs c.536A>G (p.H179R), c.590G>A (p.R197Q) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.536A>G (p.H179R). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.536A>G (p.H179R) does not impair protein function. c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*62 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.536A>G and c.590G>A are uncertain

name: *2x2
findings: CYP2D6*2x2 is assigned increased function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*2x2 was described in a family demonstrating increased enzyme activity based on urine metabolites with a debrisoquine substrate (7903454). Subjects with CYP2D6 gene duplications demonstrated increased enzyme activity with the greatest debrisoquine metabolism among all subjects (7616439, 8764380). Therefore, consensus among experts was increased function with an activity value of 2 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2D6*84 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. A subject with CYP2D6 *12/*84 demonstrated decreased dextromethorphan metabolism based on urine metabolites (28290770). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2D6*100 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*100 has been described in a subject with CYP2D6*5/*100 genotype demonstrating markedly decreased dextromethorphan metabolism compared to wildtype (23394389). CYP2D6*100 is defined by the same SNPs as CYP2D6*10 in addition to a base pair deletion resulting in a frameshift yielding a nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

name: m.827A>G
clinicalfunctionalstatus: normal risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR1 827A>G is assigned a normal risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on limited evidence. There are weak evidence proposing an association with aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss (16782057, 18261986, 20353758). However, there is one study showing patients with 827A>G mutation with a history of exposure to aminoglycosides with normal hearing (18830133) and this variant has too high a population frequency in certain haplogroups to be classified as associated with aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss. Therefore, consensus among experts was this variant is associated with normal risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on limited evidence.

NAT2*46 is assigned decreased function based on definitive evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*46 allele as "slow" . Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". NAT2*46 is characterized by the core SNVc.191G>A (p.R64Q, defining NAT2*14) which has repeatedly shown decreased NAT2 function in numerous in vivo and in vitro studies. NAT2*46 was reported in three individuals in a NAT2 phenotyping study using isoniazid (INH) as a probe drug (28959610). These individuals were classified among slow acetylators who exhibited an acetylation index (defined as the ratio of plasma INH concentration at the 3rd hour, in mg/L + 0.6 divided by the dose in mg/kg) below that of rapid acetylators, which were defined as individuals homozygous for NAT2 increased function alleles *1 or *4 (28959610). In vitro studies have shown that NAT2*46 exhibits decreased thermostability and decreased N-acetyltransferase activity toward sulfamethazine and isoniazid compared to the former reference NAT2*4 (10574910, 28523442). Six out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.191G>A (p.R64Q) impairs protein function. Published in silico modeling predicts that c.191G>A (p.R64Q) decreases protein stability and hinders electrostatic interactions (18680467).

name: *43x2
findings: CYP2D6*43x2 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. CYP2D6*43 is assigned uncertain function due to inconsistent findings regarding enzyme activity compared to wildtype (24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function as it cannot yet be determined what the phenotype of a duplicated allele with uncertain function would be clinically similar to.

There are no in vitro or clinical studies reported. Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

name: *9x2
findings: CYP2D6*9x2 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*9 is assigned decreased function based on decreased protein expression and enzyme activity (1844820, 8101460, 11266079, 24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing a duplication of this allele would demonstrate greater protein expression and enzyme activity than that of the nonduplicated allele and be clinically similar to phenotypes comprising decreased function alleles.

NAT2*65 is assigned unknown function based on a lack of evidence. NAT2*65 is characterized by the core SNVs c.613A>T (p.M205L) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.613A>T (p.M205L). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.613A>T (p.M205L) is neutral for protein function.

CYP2D6*94 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*94 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from similar to decreased activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (25469868, 26310775, 26666748, 26544071, 26406933, 27109434, 27097346, 27016952, 26542054, 26937172, 26968424, 27203132, 27251229, 27354764, 27538917). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function.

NAT2*67 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*67 is characterized by the core SNVs c.590G>A (p.R197Q), c.803G>A (p.R268K), and c.823T>A (p.L275M). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no experimental evidence describing function for c.823T>A (p.L275M). Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict c.823T>A (p.L275M) impairs protein function, which is supported by published in silico analysis (29273096). c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*67 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.823T>A and c.590G>A are uncertain.

NAT2*68 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*68 is characterized by the core SNVs c.580C>G (p.L194V), c.803G>A (p.R268K), and c.857G>A (p.G286E). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.580C>G (p.L194V). Six out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.580C>G (p.L194V) impairs protein function. c.857G>A (p.G286E) defines NAT2*7 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.857G>A (p.G286E) is neutral for protein function. However, published in silico modeling indicates that c.857G>A (p.G286E) disrupts the size/shape of the protein's active site (18680467). Thus, NAT2*68 is assigned decreased function based on c.857G>A. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.580C>G and c.857G>A are uncertain

CYP2C19*11 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. Three in vitro studies using different cell systems found CYP2C19*11 demonstrated similar enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, omeprazole, clopidogrel and other substrates (12464799, 21325430, 25001882, 21692664). Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically similar to phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

Therefore, consensus among experts for *146 and *146x2 was uncertain function.

CYP2B6*47 (c.1060_1061delinsCG, p.Y354R) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*47 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence describing function. A single in vitro study reported decreased function for p.Y354R using buproprion and efavirez as probes (38596098).

Two in vitro studies using different cell systems found CYP2C19*15 demonstrated similar enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin and omeprazole substrates (21325430, 25001882). SLCO1B1*37 is assigned normal function based on strong evidence in heterozygous and homozygous individuals. The plasma levels of highly sensitive OATP1B1 endogenous substrates GCDCA-3G, GDCA-3G and CPI are slightly and non-significantly lower in SLCO1B1*37 homozygotes (32961594, 32961019). In vitro studies have mostly demonstrated unaltered activity compared to SLCO1B1*1 (11477075).
CYP2D6*86 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

name: Amsterdam
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

TPMT*5 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*5 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (9246020). In vitro studies using mammalian cell expression systems found that TPMT.5 protein expression is decreased and TPMT.5 is degraded more rapidly compared to TPMT.1 (16220112). Additionally, TPMT.5 enzymatic activity is undetectable in vitro (16220112, 18708949). Nine out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*5-defining SNV, c.146T>C (p.L49S), impairs protein function.

CYP2D6*51 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*51 had nondetectable enzyme activity with multiple substrates (18784265, 24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

NAT2*4 is assigned increased function based on definitive evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee considered NAT2*4 as the reference allele for over two decades before the transfer of NAT2 allele nomenclature to PharmVar, which uses the GRCh38 (NC_000008.11) reference sequence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the reference NAT2*4 allele as "rapid". Under the CPIC allele function terminology, rapid acetylation alleles are categorized as "Increased Function". NAT2*4 has been studied for more than 25 years and numerous clinical and in vitro studies have demonstrated rapid acetylation by NAT2*4 with various substrates. Early clinical studies demonstrate rapid acetylation of caffeine metabolites in individuals homo- or heterozygous for the NAT2*4 allele (8102597, 7668286, 8923605, 9891107). Additional clinical studies demonstrate rapid acetylation by NAT2*4 using isoniazid (2376572, 9321764, 15565348) and sulfamethazine (1872889, 21946899). Individuals homo- or heterozygous for NAT2*4 metabolize hydralazine rapidly (24702251, 31257615). NAT2*4 is distinguished from NAT2*1 by the presence of the core SNV c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) has not been shown experimentally to alter NAT2 acetylation activity in vitro nor in vivo. Accordingly, nine out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.803G>A is neutral/tolerated for protein function.

NAT2*10 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*10 allele as "slow" and possibly substrate dependent. Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". NAT2*10 is characterized by the core SNVs c.499G>A (p.E167K) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly exhibited increased function in vivo and in vitro. c.499G>A; p.E167K was shown to decrease N-acetylation activity toward sulfamethazine in vitro due to decreased protein levels (17434923). Published in silico modeling predicts that c.499G>A (p.E167K) decreases hydrogen bond capacity, decreases hydrophobicity, and alters protein charge (18680467). However, nine out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.499G>A is neutral/tolerated for protein function. The lack of in vivo evidence, along with the conflicting in vitro and computational evidence, results in the assignment of uncertain function.
CYP2C9*75 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

CYP2D6*65 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*65 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (24647041, 30366777). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

TPMT*41 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*41 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (28462921, 32865889). In vitro studies using bacterial expression systems found that TPMT.41 exhibited decreased protein stability, decreased affinity for SAM, and decreased enzymatic activity compared to TPMT.1 (28462921, 32865889). Nine out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*41-defining SNV, c.719A>C (p.Y240S), impairs protein function.

NAT2*15 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*15 allele as "slow" . Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". NAT2*15 is characterized by the core SNVs c.191G>A (p.R64Q, defining NAT2*14) and c.590G>A (p.R197Q, defining NAT2*6) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly exhibited increased function in vivo and in vitro. c.191G>A and c.590G>A have both repeatedly shown decreased NAT2 acetylation in numerous clinical and in vitro studies. An in vitro study using a bacterial expression system found that NAT2*15 exhibited significantly decreased N-acetyltransferase activity toward sulfamethazine and isoniazid and significantly decreased thermostability compared to the former reference NAT2*4 (28523442). Six and seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.191G>A (p.R64Q) and c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impair protein function, respectively. While c.191G>A and c.590G>A are both definitively attributed to decreased function individually, the combinatorial effects of having both variants present in the same allele are uncertain. Thus, NAT2*15 is assigned a moderate level of evidence.

CYP2C9*68 is assigned uncertain funciton due to inadequate evidence. The nonsynonymous variants present in CYP2C9*68 are predicted to be deleterious in SIFT and and benign in Polyphen predictions. There are no in vitro or clinical studies reported. Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2D6*69 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*69 was identified in a subject demonstrating poor dextromethorphan metabolism (18797858). CYP2D6*69 is a rare allele defined by several SNPs including two which are attributed to the decreased function in other CYP2D6 alleles. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

CYP2D6*20 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*20 was identified in a liver sample with decreased protein expression and decreased metoprolol metabolism (10471072). CYP2D6*20 is defined by a base insertion resulting in a frameshift and premature stop codon, yielding a truncated and nonfunctional enzyme. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

NAT2*27 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. NAT2*27 is characterized by the core SNVs c.589C>T and c.803G>A. c.803G>A defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly exhibited increased function in vivo and in vitro. c.589C>T results in the amino acid change p.R197X, introducing a premature stop codon leading to the expression of a truncated protein product that is predicted to be nonfunctional (19164093, 29273096). Four out of four variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.589C>T (p.R197X) impairs function. While in silico predictions indicate that NAT2*27 exhibits decreased or possibly no enzymatic activity, the committee could not come to a consensus given the lack of in vitro or in vivo data to support the function.

NAT2*29 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*29 allele as "slow" . Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". NAT2*29 is characterized by the functional SNVs c.191G>A (p.R64Q, defining NAT2*14) and c.341T>C (p.I114T, defining NAT2*5) which have both repeatedly shown decreased NAT2 N-acetylation in numerous in vivo and in vitro studies. A NAT2 phenotyping study showed that an individual genotyped as NAT2*14/*29 exhibited decreased acetylation of dapsone, with values below the cut-off value used to discriminate phenotypic rapid from slow acetylators (10762005). An in vitro study using a bacterial expression system found that NAT2*29 exhibits decreased thermostability and decreased N-acetyltransferase activity toward sulfamethazine and isoniazid (28523442 ). An additional in vitro study showed that NAT2*5 (which contains the core SNV c.341T>C, p.I114T) is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*29 would presumably be degraded in the same manner. Additionally, five and six out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C (p.I114T) and c.191G>A (p.R64Q) impair protein function, respectively. While c.191G>A and c.341T>C are both definitively attributed to decreased function individually, the combinatorial effects of having both variants present in the same allele are uncertain.

CYP2D6*14 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Three subjects with CYP2D6*1/*14, CYP2D6 *10/*14, and CYP2D6*14/*41 genotype, respectively demonstrated decreased dextromethorphan metabolism compared to wildtype based on urine metabolites (19364831). Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*14 had modestly decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (18784265, 19364831, 24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

name: c.2195T>G

NAT2*30 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*30 allele as "slow" . Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". NAT2*30 is characterized by the core SNVs: c.341T>C (p.I114T), 590G>A (p.R197Q), and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly exhibited increased function in vivo and in vitro. c.341T>C (defining NAT2*5) and c.590G>A (defining NAT2*6) have both repeatedly shown decreased NAT2 N-acetylation in numerous in vivo and in vitro studies. NAT2*30 was reported in a clinical NAT2 phenotyping study using caffeine as a probe drug; 4 individuals heterozygous for NAT2*30 exhibited decreased acetylation of caffeine metabolites (15558239 ). An additional in vitro study showed that NAT2*5 (which contains the core SNV c.341T>C, p.I114T) is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*30 would presumably be degraded in the same manner. Five and seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C (p.I114T) and c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impair protein function, respectively, while nine out of nine predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.803G>A (p.R268K) is neutral/tolerated for protein function. Though c.341T>C and:c.590G>A are both definitively attributed to decreased function individually, the combinatorial effects of having both variants present in the same allele are uncertain.

TPMT*10 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. An individual genotyped TPMT*1/*10 was prescribed 50 mg/day 6-MP and experienced cytopenia after 3 months of treatment (10833476). In vitro studies found TPMT.10 protein expression is comparable to TPMT.1 in mammalian cells (16220112, 18708949) and yeast cells (13679074). TPMT.10 enzymatic activity is decreased by 30-70% in vitro relative to TPMT.1 (13679074, 16220112, 18708949) and TPMT.10 exhibited decreased affinity for 6-TG (18708949). Six out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*10-defining SNV, c.430G>C (p.G144R), impairs protein function.

name: Nara
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

NAT2*32 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*32 is characterized by the core SNVs c.203G>A (p.C68Y) and c.341T>C (p.I114T). An in vitro study showed that substituting a glycine in place of cysteine at position p.68 abolished NAT2 N-acetyltransferase activity toward sulfamethazine, procainamide, p-aminosalicylic acid, and 2-aminofluorene; the authors discuss the high probability that p.Cys68 lies within the NAT2 active site and is directly involved in acetyl transfer (1559981). An additional in vitro study found that NAT2 p.C68A exhibited undetectable N-acetyltransferase activity toward p-aminosalicylic acid and sulfamethazine (9173883). Thus, substitution of a tyrosine in place of cysteine at residue p.68 would likely abolish NAT2 enzymatic activity. Published in silico modeling predicts that c.203G>A (p.C68Y) alters the NAT2 catalytic mechanism, rendering the protein non-functional (19909761). Lastly, nine out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.203G>A (p.C68Y) impairs protein function. c.341T>C (p.I114T) defines NAT2*5, which has repeatedly shown decreased NAT2 acetylation activity in numerous in vivo and in vitro studies. Additionally, an in vitro study showed that NAT2*5 is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*32 would presumably be degraded in the same manner. While the committee agreed to the assignment of decreased function based on the function of c.341T>C (p.I114T), there was a lack of consensus regarding the function of c.203G>A (p.C68Y). It remains unknown whether there are any combinatorial effects associated with c.341T>C and c.203G>A.

NAT2*34 is assigned decreased function based on definitive evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*34 allele as "slow" . Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". NAT2*34 is characterized by the core SNV c.590G>A (p.R197Q) which has been studied for more than 25 years and has shown decreased NAT2 N-acetylation activity in numerous in vivo and in vitro studies using various substrates. In vivo phenotyping studies have demonstrated that individuals heterozygous for NAT2*34 in combination with another decreased function allele exhibit decreased N-acetylation activity toward caffeine metabolites (15558239, 17011540) and isoniazid (9154882, 28959610). Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. In vitro studies have shown that NAT2*34 exhibits decreased thermostability and decreased N-acetyltransferase activity toward sulfamethazine and isoniazid (28523442).

CYP2C9*65 is assigned uncertain funciton due to inadequate evidence. The nonsynonymous variant present in CYP2C9*65 is predicted to be deleterious in both SIFT and Polyphen predictions. There are no in vitro or clinical studies reported. Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2B6*15 (c.1172T>A, p.I391N) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*15 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using human liver microsomes (17559344, 15190123) and cDNA expression systems (15190123, 20517174, 21746968, 30201214) showed decreased to no enzyme activity for CYP2B6*15 compared to the *1 reference (20662624). However, due to the lack of any in vivo or clinical evidence, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on limited evidence.

CYP2C19*19 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C19*19 had decreased intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, omeprazole, and clopidogrel substrates (17455109, 21325430, 25001882). Although two in vitro studies found CYP2C19*19 demonstrated similar intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with omeprazole and mephenytoin, the experts did not find these results convincing as CYP2C19*19 also demonstrated higher Km values compared to wildtype in these studies, suggesting CYP2C19*19 has decreased substrate affinity (18312490, 21325430). Additionally, decreased intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with these two substrates was previously reported as mentioned above. Another study found CYP2C19*19 exhibited significantly reduced catalytic activities compared with CYP2C19*1 across multiple substrates (21692664).Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2C9*34 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. Although one subject with CYP2C9*1/*34 genotype exhibited decreased clearance compared to wildtype subjects based on tolbutamide plasma concentrations after one 500mg dose (25614704), in vitro studies found CYP2C9*34 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from decreased to increased compared to wildtype with various substrates (23752738, 18971529, 23844998, 19541829, 24118918, 25142737, 25476996, 25614704, 25832633, 25884291, 25924705, 27163851, 24077631, 25144335, 25951663). Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned..

CYP2D6*61 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies have found CYP2D6*61 demonstrated decreased to no detectable enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (24647041, 30366777) but consensus among experts was uncertain function.

NAT2*35 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*35 is characterized by the core SNVs c.152G>T (p.G51V), c.590G>A (p.R197Q, defining NAT2*6), and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. Published in silico modeling predicts that c.152G>T (p.G51V) alters substrate binding (19909761). Four out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.152G>T (p.G51V) impairs NAT2 protein function. c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*35 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A (p.R197Q). Despite this, it remains unknown whether there are any combinatorial effects associated with c.152G>T and c.590G>A.

NUDT15*13 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. The NUDT15*13 SNV (NM_018283.4:c.343dup) results in a frameshift at amino acid residue 115 and the loss of the enzymatic domain (29519865). Patients carrying a NUDT15*13 allele had a 33% reduction in thiopurine dosage following the occurrence of adverse reactions (29519865). NUDT15*13 is characterized by the duplication c.343_344insG (p.E115fs), which is predicted to impair protein function by CADD and Phd-SNPg

NAT2*71 is assigned unknown function based on a lack of evidence. NAT2*71 is defined by c.704T>G (p.V235G) for which there is no literature describing function. Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.704T>G (p.V235G) impairs protein function.

NAT2*74 is assigned unknown function based on a lack of evidence. NAT2*74 is defined by the core SNVs c.803G>A (p.R268K) and c.845A>G (p.K282R). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.845A>G (p.K282R). Three out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.845A>G (p.K282R) impairs protein function.

CYP2C19*12 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In one in vitro study CYP2C19*12 was poorly expressed and enzyme activity could not be assessed with a mephenytoin substrate (12464799). CYP2C19*12 is defined by the conversion of the stop codon to cysteine, and it is unclear if this protein is unstable in vivo. Therefore, consensus among experts was not achieved and uncertain function was assigned.

name: c.11947C>T
findings: PS2_PM6_Mod, PS4_Sup, PP3_Mod, BS3_Sup

CYP2B6*2 (c.64C>T, p.R22C) - Normal function: CYP2B6*2 is assigned normal function based on in vivo (heterozygous patients) and in vitro studies. Multiple studies evaluating efavirenz plasma concentrations among patients with HIV found that CYP2B6*2 heterozygotes had similar efavirenz plasma concentrations compared to the *1 reference (21896912, 22471906, 23254426, 23399569). A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among HIV patients taking efavirenz for more than three months, similarly found that CYP2B6*2 did not impact long term efavirenz exposure (26655325). Additionally, one in vitro study using human liver microsomes (17559344) and two in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems (12642465, 30201214) found that CYP2B6*2 had similar enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on strong evidence.

SLCO1B1*16 is assigned uncertain function based on limited in vitro evidence and clinical data. One clinical study showed 2 heterozygous subjects treated with pravastatin had similar pharmacokinetic parameters to subjects with wildtype alleles and one invitro study found normal protein expression (33658230). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

SLCO1B1*33 is assigned uncertain function based on limited in vitro evidence and clinical data. One clinical study showed no statistical difference in methotrexate clearance (22147369) and one in vitro study found decreased protein expression (33658230). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

CYP2C9*39 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*39 demonstrated decreased clearance with numerous substrates compared to wildtype (24077631, 24118918, 25142737, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25951663, 25884291, 27163851, 23844998). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function or decreased function alleles.

name: Aveiro
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

TPMT*20 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*20 exhibited intermediate activity and this patient experienced azathioprine-related pancreatitis (16917910). An in vitro study using a mammalian cell expression system found that TPMT.20 exhibited decreased affinity for 6-TG and enzymatic activity was reduced by 75% compared to TPMT.1 (18708949). Two out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*20-defining SNV, c.712A>G (p.K238E), impairs protein function.

CYP2C19*24 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C19*24 demonstrated nondetectable enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, clopidogrel, and omeprazole substrates (25001882, 27578295, 31713493). Additionally, CYP2C19*24 had less holoprotein compared to wildtype suggesting the protein is less stable resulting in little to no enzyme activity (27578295). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function and decreased function alleles.

CYP2C9*66 is assigned uncertain funciton due to inadequate evidence. The nonsynonymous variant present in CYP2C9*66 is predicted to be tolerated in SIFT and benign in Polyphen predictions. There are no in vitro or clinical studies reported. Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

name: *4x2
findings: CYP2D6*4x2 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. CYP2D6*4x2 has been described in subjects demonstrating decreased metabolism of dextromethorphan or debrisoquine (10022755, 12152006). CYP2D6*4 is defined by a splicing defect resulting in a nonfunctional protein and gene duplication would therefore not impact enzyme activity. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on moderate evidence.

TPMT*40 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*40 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity, however there was no difference in thiopurine dose intensity between TPMT*1/*1 patients and one patient genotyped TPMT*1/*40 (27564568). TPMT.40 exhibited comparable protein expression to TPMT.1 in mammalian cells (29785012). Three out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*40-defining SNV, c.677G>A (p.R226Q), impairs protein function.

TPMT*18 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*18 exhibited intermediate activity (15226673). An in vitro study using a mammalian cell expression system found that TPMT.18 protein expression is decreased compared to TPMT.1 and TPMT.18 enzymatic activity is undetectable (18708949). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*18-defining SNV, c.211G>A (p.G71R) impairs protein function.

CYP2C9*11 is assigned decreased function based on definitive evidence in heterozygous patients and in vitro studies. Four subjects carrying the CYP2C9*11 allele (n=3 CYP2C9*1/*11, n=1 CYP2C9*8/*11) demonstrated decreased phenytoin metabolism compared to wildtype subjects based on urinary excretion of a phenytoin metabolite after a single 300mg dose (16220110). Three CYP2C9*1/*11 patients on a stable dose of warfarin required a 33% lower warfarin dose compared to CYP2C9*1/*1 patients which correlated with the in vitro investigation where CYP2C9*11 had low levels of enzyme expression, decreased half-life, and 70% catalytic efficiency compared to wildtype; these results are suggestive of improper protein folding leading to decreased stability and reduced enzyme activity (15970795). An examination of the crystal structure showed the substitution which defines the CYP2C9*11 allele disrupts the sole extrahelical hydrogen bonding interaction, destabilizing the structure (15284535). In vitro studies showed CYP2C9*11 demonstrated decreased metabolism of tolbutamide, warfarin, glimepiride, carvedilol, fluoxetine, flurbiprofen, mestranol, diclofenac, and losartan compared to wildtype (15284535, 23752738, 24118918, 25476996, 25884291, 27199745, 25144335, 25924705, 25951663, 23844998). Although other in vitro studies showed CYP2C9*11 had similar activity compared to wildtype, the experts did not find these results convincing as in these studies CYP2C9*2 and/or CYP2C9*3 which definitively have decreased function and no function, respectively, also demonstrated similar activity compared to wildtype in these studies (25142737, 25832633, 27163851). The experts did not find the result of an in vitro study which showed CYP2C9*11 had similar activity compared to wildtype with a tolbutamide substrate (24077631) convincing because this result was not replicated as two separate in vitro studies which used two different systems and showed CYP2C9*11 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype, as described previously (15284535, 23752738). The experts also did not deem the results of a study which found no difference in losartan metabolism among four subjects carrying the CYP2C9*11 allele nor a study which found no difference in acenocoumarol metabolism among one subject with CYP2C9*1/*11 genotype as convincing conflicting evidence due to the small sample size (15289788, 21811894). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on definitive evidence.

CYP2C9*18 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In one in vitro study, CYP2C9*18 protein expression could not be detected in an E.coli system (16099926). CYP2C9*18 protein expression was detected in a separate study using COS-7 cells and no enzyme activity was detected with warfarin and tolbutamide substrates (23752738). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to insufficient evidence.

TPMT*25 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*25 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (18602085). An in vitro study using a yeast expression system found that TPMT.25 exhibited decreased affinity for 6-TG and TPMT.25 enzymatic activity is decreased by 30% compared to TPMT.1 (18602085). Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*25-defining SNV, c.634T>C (p.C212R), impairs protein function.

CYP2D6*64 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*64 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (24647041, 30366777).
