name: c.7522C>T
findings: PS3_St, PS4_Mod, PP3_Mod
SLCO1B1*48 is assigned no function due to evidence supporting a full gene deletion (22232210). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function due to limited evidence.

TPMT*15 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from a patient genotyped TPMT*3A/*15 exhibited very low enzymatic activity (15083071, 31005613). The patient's mother was genotyped TPMT*1/*15 and exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (15083071). The TPMT*15-defining SNV, c.495-1G>A, disrupts the canonical splice site and results in the loss of exon 7, which is further supported by SpliceAI predictions. Additionally, the variant effect predictors, CADD and PhD-SNPg, predict that c.495-1G>A impairs protein function.

TPMT*24 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*24 exhibited intermediate activity (18602085, 27564568), while erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*3A/*24 or *3C/*24 exhibited low enzymatic activity (33118454). There is no difference in thiopurine dose intensity observed between individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*1 and TPMT*1/*24 (27564568, 34412101). An in vitro study using a yeast expression system found that TPMT.24 exhibited decreased affinity for 6-TG, but the rate of enzymatic activity is unaffected compared to TPMT.1 (18602085). TPMT.24 exhibited comparable protein expression to TPMT.1 in mammalian cells (29785012). One out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predicts that the TPMT*24-defining SNV, c.537G>T (p.Q179H), impairs protein function.

CYP2D6*27 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*27 demonstrated similar activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (18784265, 18838503). Although some in vitro studies found CYP2D6*27 demonstrated modestly decreased activity it was not convincing the magnitude of decreased activity was clinically significant (24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function with an activity value of 1 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically similar to phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

TPMT*44 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*44 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (31005613, 32865889). Recombinant TPMT.44 expressed in E coli. exhibited decreased protein stability (31005613) and protein abundance was low when expressed in mammalian cells (29785012). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*44-defining SNV, c.497A>G (p.Y166C), impairs protein function.

name: c.3067C>A
findings: 5-fluorouracil (ex vivo)

TPMT*45 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. A patient genotyped TPMT*1/*45 exhibited low TPMT enzymatic activity and a plasma 6-TGN concentration above the therapeutic range (33273844). Following reduction of the thiopurine dosage, the patient's 6-TGN plasma concentration decreased and was within the therapeutic range (33273844). However, another patient genotyped TPMT*1/*45 tolerated a 6-MP dose of 73.1 mg/m2 (34412101). TPMT*45 results in the generation of a premature stop codon at residue 226 and the production of a truncated protein. The premature stop occurs upstream of the known "no function" variant (c.719A>G, defining TPMT*3C), suggesting that loss of this region of the protein alters function. Two out of four variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*45-defining SNV, c.676C>T (p.R226X), impairs protein function. In a study of variant abundance by massively parallel sequencing, the TPMT*45 variant was found to have low protein abundance (29785012).

TPMT*46 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. TPMT*46 is characterized by two SNVs: c.487C>T, p.R163C (the *33 variant) and c.644G>A, p.R215H (the *8 variant). TPMT*8 is assigned decreased function and TPMT*33 is assigned no function. Nine out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.644G>A (p.R215H) is neutral for protein function and seven out of ten predict that c.487C>T (p.R163C) impairs protein function. TPMT*46 is assigned no function based on the *33 SNV (c.487C>T; p.R163C).

TPMT*47 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. TPMT*47 is characterized by a four base pair deletion in exon II (c.21_24delACTT) resulting in a frameshift of the coding sequence and the creation of a premature stop codon, which is predicted to undergo nonsense-mediated decay. The variant effect predictors, CADD and PhD-SNPg, predict that c.21_24del impairs protein function.

CYP2D6*46 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. Subjects carrying the CYP2D6*46 allele demonstrated similar dextromethorphan metabolism and tramadol pharmacokinetics compared to subjects with two functional alleles (15768052). An vitro study found CYP2D6*46 demonstrated similar activity compared to wildtype with dextromethorphan and bufuralol substrates (15768052). Although two in vitro study found CYP2D6*46 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with dextromethorphan, bufuralol, and primaquine substrates (24647041, 30366777) the experts did not find this result convincing as CYP2D6*46 demonstrated similar activity compared to wildtype in vivo and in vitro with the same substrates. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function with an activity value of 1 based on limited evidence.

CYP2D6*90 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*90 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from increased to decreased activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (25469868, 26310775, 26666748, 26544071, 26406933, 27109434, 27097346, 27016952, 26542054, 26937172, 26968424, 27203132, 27251229, 27354764, 27538917). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function.

name: c.14422_14423delinsAA
findings: PM1_Sup

SLCO1B1*45 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. SLCO1B1*45 is defined by c.1738C>T (p.R580X). This variant generates a premature stop codon resulting in the expression of a truncated protein. SLCO1B1*45 was significantly associated with increased bilirubin levels and an individual carrying SLCO1B1*45 experienced statin-associated muscle symptoms (38674010). Additionally, SLCO1B1 loss-of-function has been implicated in the pathogenesis of Rotor syndrome and SLCO1B1*45 has been identified in a homozygous state in probands (32082363, 36964102, 39071607, 25546334). Cryo-EM structural studies of SLCO1B1 protein have consistently identified p.R580 as a critical amino acid involved in substrate recognition and binding (40199401, 37674011). The SLCO1B1 protein contains 12 transmembrane domains; a truncation at p.R580 eliminates the final two transmembrane domains which is highly disruptive rendering the protein non-functional, as the full transmembrane architecture is required for substrate transport. Four out of four VEPs capable of making predictions for nonsense variants predict that c.1738C>T impairs protein function.

CYP2C19*31 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C19*31 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, omeprazole, voriconazole, and nebivolol substrates (26153442, 30464555, 29098786). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse and not consistent among substrates. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2D6*111 is assigned uncertain function. CYP2D6*111 is defined by a SNP which in silico studies predict to be a deleterious amino acid change (31368850). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2B6*7 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R and c.1459C>T, p.R487C) - Decreased function: CYB2B6*7 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*7 contains the variants which define CYP2B6*6 in addition to a third SNP (c.1459C>T). Since this is a rare variant, there are no data from human liver microsomes or clinical trials regarding its function. Data from several in vitro expression systems are inconclusive: some studies show decreased activity (31324697, 32238417, 3553863) while others exhibit increased activity (12642465, 21659470, 30201214). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2D6*71 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*71 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (24647041, 30366777). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

name: Abeno
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

TPMT*6 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*6 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity toward 6-MP (9246020). An individual carrying TPMT*6 experienced severe leukopenia following standard azathioprine dosing (39696474). In vitro studies using mammalian cell expression systems demonstrate that TPMT.6 protein expression is substantially decreased compared to TPMT.1 (16220112). Additionally, TPMT.6 exhibited reduced substrate affinity for 6-TG, reduced clearance of 6-TG and 6-MP, and enzymatic activity was decreased by 60-80% in vitro (16220112, 18708949). Recombinant TPMT.6 expressed and purified from bacterial cells exhibited decreased protein stability, however activity was unaffected when using 6-MP as a substrate (30023734). Despite conflicting in vitro evidence, the experts agreed to assign no function based on the available clinical data. Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*6-defining SNV, c.539A>T (p.Y180F), impairs protein function.

CYP2C19*10 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence.Six in vitro studies using different cell systems found CYP2C19*10 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, omeprazole, and clopidogrel substrates (12464799, 19661214, 21325430, 25001882, 24945780). Another study found CYP2C19*10 exhibited significantly reduced catalytic activities compared with CYP2C19*1 across multiple substrates (21692664). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2B6*9 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect) - Decreased function: CYP2B6*9 is assigned decreased function. When c.516G>T occurs alone, it defines CYP2B6*9. The c.516G>T variant causes aberrant splicing (14551287, 18171905, 24260284) and is in high linkage disequilibrium with c.785A>G, which is part of the CYP2B6*6 haplotype. c.516G>T is also present in several other haplotypes (e.g., CYP2B6*7, *13, *19, *20, *26, *34, *36, *37 and *38). These haplotypes make it difficult to ascertain the presence of *9 and its clinical function. Although several studies have reported a significant association between c.516G>T and efavirenz exposure (over 3-fold increase) in patient plasma and/or hair samples (15622315, 22927450, 23080225), these associations were erroneously attributed to CYP2B6*9 rather than *6. Several in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems show that CYP2B6*9 decreases activity towards efavirenz, bupropion, methadone, ketamine, and nicotine (21659470, 30085944, 30906561, 32238417, 35538637), while others have shown no or increased activity (11243870, 30201214). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. At present, functional information is informed by the fact that alleles with c.516G>T (e.g., CYP2B6*6) have been associated with decreased function (see description for CYP2B6*6 allele). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on strong evidence.

CYP2B6*26 (c.499C>G, p.P167A; c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*26 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*26 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information). One study evaluating efavirenz plasma concentrations among patients with HIV found CYP2B6*26 was associated with elevated efavirenz concentrations compared to the *1 reference and has similar function to CYP2B6*6. This was demonstrated by similar efavirenz concentrations among CYP2B6 *6/*26 subjects CYP2B6 *6/*6 subjects (17918089). Although one in vitro study using a cDNA expression system found that CYP2B6*26 demonstrated increased enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (30201214), the experts did not find these results convincing as it has previously been shown that in vitro expression systems may not adequately reflect CYP2B6*6 expression and function, and therefore also not be a suitable system to assess CYP2B6*26. As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2B6*40 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R; c.1133G>A, p.R378Q) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*40 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*40 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information). As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). A single in vitro study reported decreased function for p.R378Q using buproprion and efavirez as probes (38596098). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

name: Munich
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

name: c.12121C>T
findings: BP4

NAT2*1 is assigned increased function based on definitive evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the reference NAT2*1 allele as "rapid". Under the CPIC allele function terminology, rapid acetylation alleles are categorized as "Increased Function". NAT2*1 corresponds to the NCBI reference sequence, NG_012246.1, containing no SNVs. NAT2*1 has been studied for more than 25 years and has shown rapid acetylation in numerous clinical studies with various substrates. Rapid acetylation activity has been demonstrated in individuals heterozygous for NAT2*1 using caffeine (9333101, 10744128, 15558239, 20801937, 24221535, 27223070), dapsone (10762005), sulfamethazine (21946899), and isoniazid (30149019). NAT2*1 is distinguished from NAT2*4 by the absence of the core SNV c.803G>A (p.R268K).

name: c.14210G>A
findings: PS4, PM1_Sup, PP1_St, PP3_Mod, BS2_Mod, PS3_Mod

CYP2B6*42 (c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R; c.1328G>A, p.R443H) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*42 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*42 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information). As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). A single in vitro study reported decreased function for p.R443H using buproprion and efavirez as probes (38596098). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

name: c.13990T>C
findings: PS4_Sup, PM1_Sup, PP3_Mod

CYP2C19*16 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. A subject with CYP2C19 *2/*16 genotype demonstrated decreased mephobarbital excretion compared to heterozygous CYP2C19 *2 or CYP2C19*3 subjects but greater mephobarbital excretion compared to subjects carrying two no function alleles based on urine metabolites after a 200mg dose (15499191). Two in vitro studies using different cell systems found CYP2C19*16 had nondetectable or decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, omeprazole, and clopidogrel substrates (25001882, 21325430). Another study found CYP2C19*16 exhibited significantly reduced catalytic activities compared with CYP2C19*1 across multiple substrates (21692664).Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.
SLCO1B1*43 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

TPMT*39 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*39 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity and the patient was started on a reduced dose of thiopurines but continued to experience side effects (27703193). TPMT.39 expressed in mammalian cells exhibited decreased protein abundance relative to TPMT.1 (29785012) Eight out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*39-defining SNV, c.218C>T (p.A73V), impairs protein function.

NAT2*59 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*59 is characterized by the core SNVs c.475T>A (p.W159R), c.803G>A (p.R268K), and c.857G>A (p.G286E). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.475T>A (p.W159R). Eight out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.475T>A (p.W159R) impairs protein function. c.857G>A (p.G286E) defines NAT2*7 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.857G>A (p.G286E) is neutral for protein function. However, published in silico modeling indicates that c.857G>A (p.G286E) disrupts the size/shape of the protein's active site (18680467). Thus, NAT2*59 is assigned decreased function based on c.857G>A. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.475T>A and c.857G>A are uncertain.

name: c.520C>T
findings: 19825159: positive in vitro contracture test (biopsied muscle strips) (IVCT); 28259615: positive IVCT; 22547813: R174W mutation does not alter the voltage dependence of EC coupling, indicating that this mutation does not shift the equilibrium of RyR1 toward the activated state. R174W mutation alters the resting conformation of CACNA1S such that it no longer suppresses resting calcium leak via RYR1 and R174W mutation also serves to amplify this resting leak. R174W mutation disrupts the ability of CACNA1S to repress resting calcium leak from SR via RYR1 leading t elevated resting myoplasmic calcium and increased sensitivity to MH triggers

CYP2C9*69 is assigned uncertain funciton due to inadequate evidence. The nonsynonymous variant present in CYP2C9*69 is predicted to be deleterious in both SIFT and Polyphen predictions. There are no in vitro or clinical studies reported. Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.
15179405: SNS-38 (in vivo) - based on protein quantification

name: Calvo Mackenna
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

TPMT*43 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. TPMT*43 results in a splicing defect and the loss of exon 5, producing a non-functional enzyme (31005613, 32865889). Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*43 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (31005613, 32865889). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*43-defining SNVs, c.234G>T (p.W78C) and c.262G>A (p.G88S), impair protein function.

CYP2C9*61 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence in a heterozygous patient. CYP2C9*61 was first described in a patient requiring a low daily warfarin dose of 3mg (30518301). CYP2C9*61 is defined by two SNPs, one of which is the defining SNP for CYP2C9*2. Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the function of this allele is similar to CYP2C9*2 and phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2D6*147 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. CYP2D6*147 is defined by the insertion of three amino acids found in CYP2D6*30 (for which the function is uncertain) and the 100C>T variant which has been shown to cause severely decreased function of e.g. *10. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function.

name: Cairo
findings: WHO Class II assigned based on evidence supporting decreased enzyme activity (less than A-). Additionally, analysis of the clinical presentation of the 11 children with G6PD Cairo revealed that all suffered hemolytic crises due to fava bean consumption and reported varying levels of jaundice at birth. The resulting in a WHO class II assignment (22770933).

CYP2C9*5 is assigned decreased function based on strong evidence in heterozygous patients and in vitro studies. Three subjects carrying the CYP2C9*5 allele (CYP2C9*1/*5, CYP2C9*5/*6, CYP2C9*5/*8 genotype) had decreased losartan metabolism compared to wildtype based on urinary metabolites after a single 25mg dose (15289788). Two CYP2C9 *1/*5 subjects, one CYP2C9 *5/*6 subject, and one CYP2C9*5/*8 subject had lower phenytoin metabolism compared to wildtype based on urinary excretion of a phenytoin metabolite after a single 300mg dose. Additionally, the CYP2C9 *5/*6 subject demonstrated the lowest phenytoin metabolism as the only subject of the 109 subject cohort to have a urinary metabolite concentration below the limit of quantification (16220110). One CYP2C9 *5/*8 demonstrated the lowest acenocoumarol metabolism based on plasma concentrations in a 36 subject cohort (21811894). One CYP2C9*1/*5 subject demonstrated decreased indisulam metabolism with an AUC nearly two times greater than wildtype subjects (17504998). These results correlated with in vitro pharmacokinetic studies which demonstrated decreased clearance of warfarin, diclofenac, flurbiprofen, naproxen, and piroxicam compared to wildtype (11455026, 11901091). A separate in vitro study found CYP2C9*5 has decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with warfarin and tolbutamide substrates that (23752738). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on strong evidence.

CYP2C19*3 is assigned no function based on definitive evidence. CYP2C19*3 has been studied for more than 20 years and its association with impaired function is well-established (7969038, 9103550, 22027650). CYP2C19*3 is defined by an early stop codon resulting in a truncated protein lacking a heme binding region and a majority of the substrate binding region, making it a nonfunctional protein. CYP2C19*3 was first identified in a homozygous subject demonstrating decreased mephenytoin metabolism and was subsequently identified in six additional subjects demonstrating decreased mephenytoin metabolism (7969038).
15179405: SNS-38 (in vivo) - based on protein quantification

CYP2D6*39 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. A liver microsomes sample of a subject with CYP2D6 *10/*39 genotype demonstrated similar enzyme activity compared to wildtype with a bufuralol substrate (11266079). Similarly, in vitro studies found CYP2D6*39 demonstrated similar enzyme activity compared to wildtype with bufuralol, dextromethorphan, and tamoxifen substrates (18784265, 24647041). Although one in vitro study found CYP2D6*39 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype with a primaquine substrate (30366777) the experts did not find this result convincing as CYP2D6*39 demonstrated similar activity compared to wildtype towards a majority of substrates. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function with an activity value of 1 based on limited evidence.

CYP2D6*44 is assigned no function based on limited evidence in a heterozygous subject. CYP2D6*44 was identified in a subject with CYP2D6 *2/*44 genotype demonstrating decreased dextromethorphan metabolism compared to CYP2D6 *1/*2 subjects (15618746). CYP2D6*44 is defined by a splicing defect yielding a nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

CYP2D6*38 is assigned no function based on limited evidence in a heterozygous subject. CYP2D6*38 was identified in a heterozygous subject with CYP2D6 *4/*38 genotype demonstrating poor metabolism with a debrisoquine substrate (9918137). CYP2D6*38 is defined by a four base pair deletion resulting in a frameshift. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

name: Coimbra Shunde
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

CYP2B6*29 (CYP2B7-CYP2B6 hybrid gene) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*29 is assigned decreased function. This allele is a hybrid consisting of CYP2B7 (exons 1-4) and CYP2B6 (exons 5-9) harboring 23 amino acid changes compared to the protein encoded by the CYP2B6*1 reference. Among those is c.516G>T which is one of the function-altering variants defining CYP2B6*6. CYP2B6*29 was identified in a subject with an elevated efavirenz AUC compared to the *1 reference and similar to those seen in patients carrying one decreased function allele (17885627). One in vitro study found that primary human hepatocytes of subjects with a CYP2B6*29 allele had slightly lower protein expression and enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (28181240). The same study also showed that the AUC for 4-OH-cyclophosphamide was significantly lower for patients with a CYP2B6*1/*29 genotype compared to *1/*1 (28181240). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on limited evidence.

name: Insuli
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

CYP2C9*6 is assigned no function based on strong supporting evidence in heterozygous and homozygous patients. This allele was first identified in a CYP2C9*6/*6 patient experiencing phenytoin toxicity after recently starting the medication. Based on serial phenytoin plasma concentrations after discontinuing phenytoin, the patient had an estimated 17% phenytoin clearance compared to wildtype (11740344). In a separate case report, a patient with CYP2C9*6/*6 genotype stable on a low weekly warfarin dose demonstrated impaired warfarin metabolism with an S:R warfarin ratio 6x greater than expected (15094935). The S:R warfarin ratio of this patient was the highest among 52 patients on a stable dose of warfarin with a CYP2C9 genotype of *1/*1, *1/*2, *1/*3, *2/*2, or *2/*3 (17895500). Two subjects (CYP2C9*1/*6 and CYP2C9*5/*6 genotype, respectively) had decreased losartan metabolism compared to wildtype subjects based on urinary metabolites after a single 25mg dose (15289788). Three CYP2C9*1/*6 subjects and one CYP2C9*5/*6 subjects had lower phenytoin metabolism compared to wildtype based on urinary excretion of a phenytoin metabolite after a single 300mg dose. Additionally, the CYP2C9*5/*6 subject demonstrated the lowest phenytoin metabolism as the only subject of the 109 subject cohort to have a urinary metabolite concentration below the limit of quantification (16220110). CYP2C9*6 is defined by a deletion resulting in a frameshift and not included in in vitro studies as it is it is a null allele resulting in no protein formation (23752738). Although one CYP2C9*1/*6 subject had a similar acenocoumarol metabolite plasma concentration compared to wildtype subjects 8 and 24 hours after a single 8mg dose (21811894), the experts did not deem this result as convincing conflicting evidence based on the small sample size. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on strong evidence.

name: Kambos
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

CYP2C19*32 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C19*32 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from decreased to similar activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (26153442, 30464555, 29098786). Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2C9*12 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence in heterozygous patients and in vitro studies. Four patients on a stable dose of warfarin with CYP2C9*1/*12 genotype required a lower weekly warfarin dose compared to CYP2C9*1/*1 patients (23688605). In vitro studies similarly found CYP2C9*12 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity with warfarin and tolbutamide substrates compared to wildtype (23752738, 15284535, 28758225). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2C9*14 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence in heterozygous patients and in vitro studies. Separate studies of patients on a stable dose of warfarin with CYP2C9 *1/*14 genotype required a lower warfarin dose compared to CYP2C9*1/*1 patients (24956244, 19077919, 15371982). The VKORC1 and CYP4F2 variants associated with increased warfarin dose requirements were also observed in one of these patients which further suggested decreased warfarin metabolism and warfarin dose was due to the CYP2C9 *1/*14 genotype (24956244). In vitro studies similarly found CYP2C9*14 demonstrated decreased clearance of warfarin, phenytoin, tolbutamide, glimepiride, carvedilol, propofol, flurbiprofen, mestranol, diclofenac, and losartan (23752738, 27163851, 16099926, 24077631, 24118918, 25476996, 25832633, 25144335, 25924705, 25951663, 23844998). Although two in vitro studies showed CYP2C9*14 had similar activity compared to wildtype, the experts did not find these results convincing as in these studies CYP2C9*2 and/or CYP2C9*3 which definitively have decreased function and no function, respectively, demonstrated increased activity compared to wildtype in these studies (25142737, 25884291). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on moderate evidence.

findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

CYP2C19*2 is assigned no function based on definitive evidence. CYP2C19*2 has been studied for more than 20 years and its association with impaired function is well-established (8195181, 22027650). CYP2C19*2 is defined by splicing defects resulting in a truncated protein lacking a heme binding region, making it a nonfunctional protein. CYP2C19*2 was first identified in a homozygous subject demonstrating decreased mephenytoin metabolism and was subsequently identified in seventeen additional subjects demonstrating decreased mephenytoin metabolism (8195181). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on definitive evidence.

CYP2B6*20 (c.503C>T, p.T168I; c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*20 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*20 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information). Three in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found decreased enzyme activity of CYP2B6*20 compared to the *1 reference with various substrates (16272958, 21746968, 30201214). As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2C9*16 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence in a heterozygous patient and in vitro studies. One subject with CYP2C9*1/*16 genotype exhibited decreased clearance compared to wildtype subjects based on tolbutamide plasma concentrations after one 500mg dose. This result correlated with the in vitro study where CYP2C9*16 demonstrated similar enzyme expression but 3.4% enzyme activity with a tolbutamide substrate compared to wildtype (25614704). Multiple in vitro studies similarly found CYP2C9*16 demonstrated decreased clearance with various substrates compared to wildtype (16099926, 24077631, 24118918, 24464553, 25951663, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25924705, 27163851, 23844998). Although one in vitro study showed CYP2C9*16 had similar activity compared to wildtype, the experts did not find this result convincing as in this study CYP2C9*2 and CYP2C9*3 which definitively have decreased function and no function, respectively, demonstrated increased activity compared to wildtype (25142737). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2C9*27 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. One subject with CYP2C9 *1/*27 genotype exhibited similar clearance compared to wildtype subjects based on tolbutamide plasma concentrations after one 500mg dose (25614704). In vitro studies showed conflicting results with CYP2C9*27 exhibiting enzyme activity ranging from decreased to increased compared to wildtype with various substrates (24118918, 23844998, 25476996, 25832633, 24077631, 25144335, 25951663, 23752738, 16788382, 25884291, 25614704, 25924705, 27163851, 25142737). Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

name: No name
findings: Function assigned based on evidence supporting complete loss of enzyme activity (32387609)

CYP2C9*41 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence in one heterozygous subject and in vitro studies. One subject with CYP2C9 *1/*41 genotype exhibited increased AUC but similar oral clearance compared to wildtype subjects based on tolbutamide plasma concentrations after one 500mg dose (25614704). In vitro studies found CYP2C9*41 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from decreased to increased compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25142737, 25144335, 25951663, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25924705, 27163851, 23844998, 25614704). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.
