findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

NUDT15*20 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. An in vitro study expressed NUDT15*20 in HEK293 cells and found NUDT15 protein expression and thiopurine sensitivity were comparable to cells expressing NUDT15*1 (32094176). Eight out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the NUDT15*20-defining SNV (c.386C>G (p.P129R)) impairs protein function.

CYP2D6*2 is assigned normal function based on moderate evidence. CYP2D6*2 has consistently been described in subjects demonstrating similar metabolism with various CYP2D6 substrates compared to wildtype (7903454, 11037799, 12915955, 20456744). Although in vitro studies found CYP2D6*2 had enzyme activity ranging from similar to decreased activity compared to wildtype, the in vitro data was not convincing as the differences in results may be due to differences in the expression system used (11266079, 18784265, 24647041, 25469868, 26310775, 26542054, 26652007, 26937172, 26968424, 27203132, 27251229, 27354764, 27538917, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function with an activity value of 1 based on moderate evidence.

name: *2x≥3
findings: CYP2D6*2x≥3 is assigned increased function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*2x≥3 was described in a family demonstrating increased enzyme activity based on urine metabolites with a debrisoquine substrate (7903454). Subjects with CYP2D6 gene duplications demonstrated increased enzyme activity with the greatest debrisoquine metabolism among all subjects (7616439, 8764380). Therefore, consensus among experts was increased function with an activity value of ≥3 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

name: Japan, Shinagawa
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

CYP2C19*33 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C19*33 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, omeprazole, and voriconazole substrates (26153442, 30464555, 29098786). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse and not consistent among substrates. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned

CYP2C9*23 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*23 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype (23844998, 24118918, 25144335, 25476996, 23752738, 25832633, 25884291, 25924705, 24077631). Although three in vitro studies showed CYP2C9*23 had similar activity compared to wildtype, the experts did not find these results convincing as in these studies CYP2C9*2 which definitively has decreased function, demonstrated increased or similar activity compared to wildtype in these studies (25142737, 25951663, 27163851). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

name: m.839A>G
clinicalfunctionalstatus: uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR1 839A>G is assigned an uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on inadequate evidence. Screening of individuals with aminoglycoside-hearing loss identified this variant in one study (21205314). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss due to insufficient evidence.

NUDT15*21 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. An in vitro study expressed NUDT15*21 in HEK293 cells and found NUDT15 protein expression and thiopurine sensitivity were comparable to cells expressing NUDT15*1 (32094176). Nine out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the NUDT15*21-defining SNV, c.410G>C (p.G137A), is neutral for protein function.

CYP2C9*48 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence in one heterozygous subject and in vitro studies. One subject with CYP2C9 *1/*48 genotype exhibited increased AUC and decreased oral clearance compared to wildtype subjects based on tolbutamide plasma concentrations after one 500mg dose (25614704). In vitro studies found CYP2C9*48 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from decreased to increased compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25142737, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25951663, 25924705, 27163851, 23844998, 25614704). Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2C9*54 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C9*54 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from decreased to increased compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25142737, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25951663, 25884291, 25924705, 27163851, 23844998). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

NUDT15*1 is assigned normal function based on definitive evidence. NUDT15*1 corresponds to the reference sequence, NG_047021.1, containing no SNVs. As such, the protein encoded by this transcript is used as the reference for in vitro (26878724, 28659275, 27530327, 32094176, 34636169, 38246006, preclinical (31645647, 37488179), and clinical studies (27604507, 26878724, 34480209, 35067667, 28445187, 32372428, 33481917, 39688234). Additionally, NUDT15*1/*1 patients have been shown to tolerate standard thiopurine dosing (26878724, 32598049, 28903549, 32308129, 29720126, 36858569, 35156873, 32221476, 31507415, 27577869, 27416873, 26033531, 32873882, 39688234).

NAT2*61 is assigned unknown function based on a lack of evidence. NAT2*61 is characterized by the core SNVs c.506A>G (p.Y169C) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.506A>G (p.Y169C). Four out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.506A>G (p.Y169C) impairs protein function.

CYP2D6*32 is assigned decreased function with an activity value of 0.25 due to limited evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*32 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from nondetectable to decreased activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (24647041, 30366777). *32 also contains 2989G>A which is the core SNP of *41 with decreased function and the only other SNP of concern, 3854G>A (E410K) would not be expected to change function as the *27 allele (defined by 3854G>A (E410K)) is normal function. Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with limited evidence.

CYP2C9*15 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2C9*15 is defined by a nonsense mutation which results in a premature stop codon resulting in a significant truncation of the protein. In an in vitro study, CYP2C9*15 did not result in detectable protein expression (16099926). CYP2C9*15 was not included in subsequent in vitro studies because it is a null allele resulting in no protein formation (23752738). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

CYP2D6*9 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. CYP2D6*9 was first identified in a liver sample with decreased protein expression and enzyme activity (1844820). Subjects with CYP2D6*9 demonstrated decreased debrisoquine metabolism but greater metabolism compared to subjects with no function alleles based on urine metabolites, indicating there is not a complete loss of enzyme function (8101460). Similarly, liver microsomes of two subjects with CYP2D6*4/*9 and CYP2D6 *1/*9 genotype demonstrated decreased intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with a bufuralol substrate (11266079). Two in vitro studies found CYP2D6*9 had a modest decrease in intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype (24647041, 30366777). Although one in vitro experiment found CYP2D6*9 had similar activity compared to wildtype with various substrates, the result was not convincing as decreased protein expression in vivo likely contributes to decreased enzyme activity observed in subjects carrying the CYP2D6*9 allele (1844820). Pharmacogenomic clinical studies found the measured activity values for several substrates is reduced (0.34 (PMID 30312494), 0.18 (PMID 33792048), 0.28 (PMID 33792048), 0.22 (PMID 32599653); 0.46 PMID 33932135) and a lower activity value of 0.25 is warranted for most substrates. Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.25 based on moderate evidence.

NAT2*5 is assigned decreased function based on definitive evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*5 allele as "slow" . Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". The functional SNV c.341T>C (p.I114T) in NAT2*5 has been studied for more than 25 years and has shown decreased N-acetylation activity in numerous clinical and in vitro studies using various substrates. Clinical studies have demonstrated that individuals homozygous for NAT2*5 or heterozygous for NAT2*5 in combination with another decreased function allele exhibit decreased acetylation activity toward caffeine metabolites (8102597, 7668286, 10744128, 9891107, 20801937, 22970273, 24221535, 28653770), isoniazid (9154882, 9321764, 19336229, 35928262) sulfonamides (1872889, 15032315, 16570187, 18167504, 21946899), and dapsone (10762005). Individuals homozygous for NAT2*5 or heterozygous for NAT2*5 in combination with another decreased function allele exhibit increased hydralazine exposure (24702251, 31257615). Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C (p.I114T) impairs protein function. In vitro studies show that NAT2*5 exhibits similar thermostability to NAT2*4, which was previously used as the reference allele (8081359, 15564878, 17434923, 11337936), however NAT2*5 is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878), indicating decreased protein levels as the molecular mechanism for the decreased acetylation activity observed.

name: *6x2
findings: CYP2D6*6x2 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*6 is defined by a single nucleotide deletion resulting in a frameshift and premature stop codon yielding a truncated and nonfunctional protein (7894499). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing a duplication of this allele would be clinically similar to phenotypes comprising the nonduplicated allele.

CYP2D6*12 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*12 is defined by a nonsense mutation yielding a nonfunctional enzyme and was identified in a subject demonstrating markedly decreased sparteine metabolism (8655150, 9241659). Two in vitro studies found CYP2D6*12 had nondetectable enzyme activity with various substrates (24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

CYP2D6*95 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*95 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from similar to decreased activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (25469868, 26310775, 26666748, 26544071, 26406933, 27109434, 27097346, 27016952, 26542054, 26937172, 26968424, 27203132, 27251229, 27354764, 27538917). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function.

CYP2D6*29 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence in homozygous subjects and in vitro studies. Three subjects with CYP2D6*29/*29 genotype demonstrated decreased metabolism compared to CYP2D6*1/*29 subjects with a debrisoquine substrate (11470994). Similarly, four subjects with CYP2D6*29/*29 genotype demonstrated decreased metabolism compared to wildtype with dextromethorphan and metoprolol substrates (11823760). Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*29 had decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (11470994, 24647041, 30366777). Although one study found subjects with CYP2D6*29/*29 genotype demonstrated similar metabolism compared to wildtype with debrisoquine and codeine substrates (11823760) the experts did not find this result convincing due to the small sample size. Therefore, consensus among experts with decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on moderate evidence.

CYP2D6*31 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence in heterozygous subjects and in vitro studies. CYP2D6*31 was first identified in a subject with CYP2D6 *4/*31 genotype demonstrating markedly decreased metoprolol metabolism with an AUC more than three times greater than the AUC of wildtype subjects and similar AUC to subjects with two known nonfunctional alleles (15726636). CYP2D6*31 was described in another subject demonstrating decreased dextromethorphan metabolism based on urine metabolites (20473659). Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*31 had nondetectable or markedly decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (15726636, 24647041, 30366777). CYP2D6*31 is defined by three amino acid substitutions and an analysis of the crystal structure and docking study suggested the Arg440His substitution causes reduced reductase binding resulting in impaired enzyme activity (15726636). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on moderate evidence.

CYP2D6*52 is assigned decreased function with an activity value of 0.25 based on limited evidence. Studies found CYP2D6*52 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with tamoxifen, bufuralol, dextromethorphan, and primaquine substrates (19364831, 23897164, 24647041, 30366777). This allele has 100C>T (the *10 SNP), 3854G>A (E410K) which we believe does not change function and 4181G>C (*2 SNP) which we also believe is benign. Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with limited evidence.

CYP2D6*75 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*75 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (26652007, 24647041, 30366777). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2D6*101 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*101 has been described in a subject with CYP2D6*2/*101 genotype demonstrating markedly decreased dextromethorphan metabolism compared to wildtype (23394389). CYP2D6*101 is defined by the same SNPs as CYP2D6*10 in addition to a 19 base pair deletion resulting in a frameshift yielding a nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

CYP2D6*120 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*120 is defined by an early stop codon yielding a truncated and nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

NAT2*33 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*33 is characterized by the core SNVs c.341T>C (p.I114T, defining NAT2*5) and c.472A>C (p.I158L). Published in silico modeling predicts that c.472A>C (p.I158L) does not alter NAT2 protein function (19164093, 19909761). Similarly, ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.472A>C (p.I158L) is neutral/tolerated for NAT2 function. Thus, NAT2*33 is assigned decreased function based on c.341T>C (p.I114T) . c.341T>C (p.I114T) defines NAT2*5 which has repeatedly shown decreased NAT2 function in numerous in vivo and in vitro studies. Additionally, an in vitro study showed that NAT2*5 is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*33 would presumably be degraded in the same manner. Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C (p.I114T) impairs protein function. While.472A>C is not predicted to impair protein function, it remains unknown whether there are any combinatorial effects associated with c.341T>C and c.472A>C.

NAT2*69 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*69 is characterized by the core SNVs c.341T>C (p.I114T) and c.589C>T (p.R197X). c.589C>T results in the amino acid change p.R197X, introducing a premature stop codon leading to the expression of a truncated protein product that is predicted to be nonfunctional (19164093, 29273096). Four out of four variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.589C>T (p.R197X) impairs function. c.341T>C (p.I114T) defines NAT2*5 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. In vitro studies also show that NAT2*5 (which contains the core SNV c.341T>C, p.I114T) is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*69 would presumably be degraded in the same manner. Additionally, five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C (p.I114T) impairs protein function. NAT2*69 is assigned decreased function based on c.341T>C. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.341T>C and c.589C>T are uncertain.

NUDT15*4 is assigned no function based on strong evidence. Clinical studies have found that NUDT15*1/*4 or NUDT15*2/*4 patients are started on lower doses of thiopurines and exhibit a greater incidence of leukopenia and therapy interruption compared to NUDT15*1/*1 patients (28903549, 36858569). Retrospective analysis found that NUDT15*1/*4 patients tolerated lower thiopurine doses (26878724, 39688234) and exhibited increased levels of dose-normalized DNA-TG in leukocytes compared to NUDT15*1/*1, *1/*2, and *1/*3 patients (39688234). In vitro evidence shows that NUDT15.4 exhibits decreased enzymatic activity (26878724, 33753169), decreased protein expression (32094176) and decreased thermostability (26878724, 28088792, 33753169) compared to NUDT15.1. Published structural modeling indicates that the functional SNV (c.416G>A (p.R139H)) disrupts the α-helix loop and the base of the substrate binding pocket (28088792). These structural destabilizing effects were confirmed using an experimentally derived crystal structure of NUDT15.4 (33753169). Five out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the NUDT15*4-defining SNV, c.416G>A (p.R139H), impairs protein function .

NUDT15*17 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. The NUDT15*17 SNV (c.352G>T) results in a premature stop codon(p.E118X) within the Nudix hydrolase domain. Additionally, the stop occurs upstream of c.415C>T, a known "no function" variant. Four out of four variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the NUDT15*17 -defining SNV (c.352G>T (p.E118X)) impairs protein function.

SLCO1B1*9 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. SLCO1B1*9 is defined by c.1463G>C (p.G488A). In vitro studies demonstrate that protein encoded by SLCO1B1*9 exhibits decreased uptake of estrone sulfate, estradiol 17beta-d-glucuronide, rifampin, rosuvastatin, methotrexate, and atrasentan (11477075, 12490595, 16697742, 16513443, 40047317). A large clinical study demonstrated that individuals with the c.1463G>C variant exhibited significantly increased bilirubin levels relative to those without. Additionally, c.1463G>C was significantly associated with statin-induced myotoxicity (40047317) and has been linked to altered oxylipin levels (39644095). Since c.1463G>A is in strong linkage with c.388A>G (p.N130D) in African populations, the association study (40047317) likely included subjects with either the SLCO1B1*9 or *31 allele Nine out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the SLCO1B1*9-defining variant, c.1463G>C (p.G488A), impairs protein function.

CYP2D6*49 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Subjects with CYP2D6 *1/*49 genotypes demonstrated decreased dextromethorphan metabolism compared to wildtype subjects and subjects with CYP2D6 *10/*49 genotype demonstrated decreased dextromethorphan metabolism compared to subjects with CYP2D6 *1/*10 genotype (19364831). Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*49 had decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with multiple substrates (18784265, 19158312, 24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence.

name: c.10097G>A
findings: BS1

name: c.12149C>A
findings: PS4_Mod, PP1_St, PP3_Mod

name: c.13918A>G
findings: PS4_Sup, PM1_Sup, PP3_Mod

name: c.14477C>T
findings: PS3, PS4, PM1_Sup, PP1_St, PP3_Mod

name: c.14545G>A
findings: PS3_Mod, PS4, PM1_Sup, PP1_St

name: c.10229C>A
findings: PP3_Mod

SLCO1B1*14 is assigned increased function based on strong evidence in heterozygous and homozygous patients. The lipid-lowering response to fluvastatin is associated with the *14 allele, with greater response in the *14/*14 patients (18781850). The clearance of high-dose methotrexate is significantly faster in patients with two *14 alleles (22147369), and response to low-dose methotrexate is reduced in patients with arthritis (31777781). Exposure to atorvastatin is decreased in heterozygous and homozygous subjects (23311897). The plasma levels of highly sensitive OATP1B1 endogenous substrate GCDCA-3G are significantly lower in *14 carriers than in individuals with the SLCO1B1*1/*1 diplotype (32961594). The *14 allele is associated with increased expression of the transporter in the liver (23311897, 24122874). In vitro studies that normalize the drug uptake to the protein concentration find no increased uptake of rifampin, estrone sulfate, or estradiol 17beta-glucuronide (11477075) and one found decreased uptake of estrone sulfate and ceravistatin (23652407). Therefore, consensus among experts was increased function.

SLCO1B1*20 is assigned increased function based on limited in vitro evidence and limited clinical data. Small clinical studies of pravastatin and atorvastatin showed similar pharmacokinetic parameters to subjects with wildtype alleles (15226675, 23311897). One clinical study found significantly increased methotrexate clearance (22147369). The plasma levels of highly sensitive OATP1B1 endogenous substrate GCDCA-3G are significantly lower in *14 carriers than in individuals with the SLCO1B1*1/*1 diplotype (32961594). Therefore, consensus among experts was increased function due to inadequate evidence.

SLCO1B1*27 is assigned uncertain function based on limited in vitro evidence and clinical data. One clinical study showed no statistical difference in methotrexate clearance (22147369) and one in vitro study found normal protein expression (33658230). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

TPMT*1 is assigned normal function based on definitive evidence. TPMT*1 corresponds to the reference sequence, NG_008807.2, containing no SNVs. As such, TPMT.1 activity is used as the reference for in vitro studies using yeast and mammalian cell expression systems (8561894, 9177237, 13679074, 16220112, 18708949). Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*1 exhibited normal to high enzymatic activity (9246020, 15226673, 23252716, 31005613). Lastly, TPMT*1/*1 patients have been shown to tolerate standard thiopurine dosing (23585236, 29387964, 33928426, 27564568, 11304783, 10580024, 19057372).

CYP2B6*5 (c.1459C>T, p.R487C) - Normal function: CYP2B6*5 is assigned normal function based on in vivo (heterozygous and homozygous patients) and in vitro studies. Multiple studies evaluating efavirenz plasma concentrations among patients with HIV found that carriers of the CYP2B6*5 allele had similar efavirenz plasma concentrations compared to the *1 reference (20662624, 20841522, 22354160, 23254426, 23399569, 23990572). A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among HIV patients taking efavirenz for more than three months found that the CYP2B6*5 allele did not impact long term efavirenz exposure (26655325). In human liver microsomes, decreased protein expression and/or activity was reported (11470993, 17559344). In in vitro expression systems, activity ranged from decreased (32238417, 31324697) to no change (21659470) and increased (12642465, 25897175) activity compared to the *1 reference with various substrates. Since differences in protein expression and enzyme activity observed in vitro were not reflected by meaningful differences in vivo in numerous clinical studies (20662624, 20841522, 22354160, 23254426, 23399569, 23990572, 26389554), consensus among experts was normal function based on strong evidence.

name: Mahidol
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

name: Nilgiri
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

SLCO1B1*31 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. SLCO1B1*31 is defined by c.388A>G (p.N130D, defining *37) and c.1463G>C (p.G488A, defining *9). SLCO1B1*37 is assigned normal function based on strong evidence as c.388A>G was not associated with altered clearance of SLCO1B1-transported drugs. SLCO1B1*9 is assigned no function based on moderate in vitro and clinical evidence. An individual genotyped SLCO1B1*1/*31 exhibited markedly decreased high-dose methotrexate clearance compared to the reference allele, and clearance was decreased compared to a SLCO1B1*5/*5 patient (22147369). An individual genotyped SLCO1B1*31/*41 exhibited an elevated plasma concentration of coproporphyrin I, at a level comparable to SLCO1B1*15/*15 patients, and this individual experienced statin-induced rhabdomyolysis (39511784). In vitro uptake of methotrexate by protein encoded by SLCO1B1*31 was significantly decreased compared to the reference; transport activity was similar to empty vector controls (22147369). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors (VEPs) tested by CPIC staff predict that c.388A>G is neutral for protein function. Nine out of ten VEPs predict that c.1463G>C impairs protein function.

CYP2B6*32 (c.1219G>A, p.A407T) – Normal function: CYP2B6*32 is assigned normal function. A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among patients with HIV taking efavirenz for more than three months found CYP2B6*32 did not impact long term efavirenz content (26655325) compared to the *1 reference. Although one in vitro study using a cDNA expression system found that CYP2B6*32 demonstrated increased enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference (30201214), the experts did not find these results convincing. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on limited evidence.

name: Toledo
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

name: *3A
findings: TPMT*3A is assigned no function based on definitive evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals heterozygous for TPMT*3A exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity, while erythrocytes from individuals homozygous for TPMT*3A or heterozygous for TPMT*3A in combination with another no function allele exhibited low enzymatic activity (9103127, 9246020, 9931346, 15226673, 23252716, 31005613). Patients carrying TPMT*3A alleles exhibited increased plasma concentrations of TGNs, decreased plasma concentrations of methylated metabolites, and tolerate lower doses of thiopurines (10580024, 11304783, 19057372, 23585236, 29387964, 33205673, 33928426). In vitro studies found that TPMT.3A protein expression and enzymatic activity is substantially decreased or undetectable relative to TPMT.1 in yeast cells (8644731, 9177237), mammalian cells (16220112,18708949, 8561894), and patient erythrocytes (9177237, 8644731, 8561894). Loss of TPMT.3A protein expression is attributed to rapid degradation by the proteasome (16220112, 9177237, 15967990). Seven and eight out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*3A-defining SNVs, c.460G>A (p.A154T) and c.719A>G (p.Y240C), impair protein function, respectively.

CYP2B6*39 (c.418C>T, p.R140W; c.516G>T, p.Q172H/splice defect; c.785A>G, p.K262R) – Decreased function: CYP2B6*39 is assigned decreased function. CYP2B6*39 contains c.516G>T which causes aberrant splicing (see CYP2B6*6 for more information). As is the same for *6, the mechanism for decreased activity is aberrant splicing caused by c.516G>T, which leads to reduced amounts of protein and thus, activity (14551287, 18171905, 24260284). As a result, alleles having c.516G>T are considered decreased function, regardless of the functional status of additional other variants unless these cause no function (e.g., CYP2B6*13 and CYP2B6*38). A single in vitro study reported decreased function for p.R140W using buproprion and efavirez as probes (38596098). Therefore, the consensus among experts was decreased function based on the presence of c.516G>T and limited evidence.

CYP2D6*17 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. CYP2D6*17 has consistently been described in heterozygous and homozygous subjects demonstrating decreased metabolism of various CYP2D6 substrates compared to wildtype subjects based on urine metabolites (8764380, 8971426, 11823760). Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*17 had at least modestly decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (9415713, 24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on moderate evidence.

name: Bangkok Noi
findings: Function assigned based on the WHO recommendations included in PMID 22293322.

NAT2*41 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. NAT2*41 is characterized by the core SNV c.578C>T (p.T193M). Published in silico modeling predicts that c.578C>T (p.T193M) impairs protein function based on exposure of hydrophobic residues that may facilitate protein aggregation or targeted degradation (19909761). Similarly, six out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.578C>T (p.T193M) impairs protein function. There are no in vitro or in vivo functional studies for NAT2*41

NAT2*45 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. NAT2*45 is characterized by the core SNV c.29T>C (p.I10T). Published in silico modeling predicts that c.29T>C (p.I10T) does not alter NAT2 protein function (19909761). Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.29T>C (p.I10T) impairs NAT2 protein function. Additionally, a structure-based computational prediction tool predicts that c.29T>C (p.I10T) decreases NAT2 protein stability which could lead to decreased function. The limited clinical data available for NAT2*45 was not sufficient to influence the allele clinical functional status (24444407).

CYP2B6*4 (c.785A>G, p.K262R) - Increased function: CYB2B6*4 is assigned increased function based on in vivo (heterozygous patients only) and in vitro studies. In clinical studies, CYP2B6*4 heterozygotes had significantly increased metabolism of several substrates such as bupropion (14515060, 29637923), nicotine (17015050) and efavirenz (17235330). Numerically higher methadone clearance was observed in CYP2B6*4 carriers, albeit samples sizes were small (26389554, 35538637). Although two studies did not find increased efavirenz clearance compared to the *1 reference (23399569, 23254426), the experts did not find these results convincing due to the small sample size. Multiple in vitro studies using cDNA expression systems showed that CYP2B6*4 has increased activity compared to the *1 reference towards efavirenz, methadone, bupropion nicotine, artemether and selegiline, (12642465, 21746968, 21821736, 31324697, 32238417). One in vitro study using cDNA expression system showed no difference in activity using bupropion as a probe (21659470), but the experts did not find this evidence convincing. Some studies found CYP2B6*4 had less activity compared to the *1 reference towards ketamine, nicotine, cyclophosphamide and ifosphamide (21821736, 21976622, 25934575, 30085944, 30906561). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. Therefore, consensus among experts was increased function based on moderate evidence, but effects may be substrate-dependent.

CYP2D6*30 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*30 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with tamoxifen, bufuralol, dextromethorphan, and primaquine substrates (24647041, 30366777). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse and not consistent among substrates. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

TPMT*9 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. Clinically, erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*9 exhibited intermediate activity (15226673, 18602085, 23252716, 30840585, 31005613). However, one individual genotyped TPMT*1/*9 exhibited normal TPMT enzymatic activity (15226673). An individual genotyped TPMT*1/*9 received 67 mg/m2 6-MP and exhibited a 6-TGN concentration of 502 pmol, which is above the therapeutic range (23252716). In vitro studies using mammalian expression systems found that TPMT.9 protein expression is comparable to TPMT.1 (16220112, 18708949). However, one study found that TPMT.9 exhibited normal enzymatic activity at ~92% of TPMT.1 activity using 6-MP as a substrate (16220112), while another found that enzymatic activity was decreased by 25% using 6-TG as a substrate (18708949). Similarly, TPMT.9 expressed in yeast cells exhibited a 25% decrease in enzymatic activity compared to TPMT.1, as well as decreased affinity for 6-TG (18602085). Molecular modeling suggests that TPMT.9 exhibited decreased protein stability (18602085). Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*9-defining SNV, c.356A>C (p.K119T), impairs protein function.

TPMT*14 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*3A/*14 and TPMT*1/*14 exhibited very low and intermediate enzymatic activity, respectively (15083071, 31005613). An in vitro study using a mammalian cell expression system found that TPMT.14 protein expression and enzymatic activity are undetectable in cells (18708949). Four out of eight variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*14-defining SNV, c.1A>G (p.M1V), impairs protein function. c.1A>G (p.M1V) is predicted to disrupt protein translation due to the loss of the canonical start codon, and there is no alternative start codon available

TPMT*21 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*21 exhibited intermediate activity (18602085, 16917910, 23252716). Additionally, a patient genotyped TPMT*1/*21 was prescribed 41 mg/m2 thioguanine and exhibited a plasma TGN concentration of 4060 pmol, which is above the therapeutic range (23252716). Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*3A/*21 had undetectable enzymatic activity (33118454). An in vitro study using a mammalian cell expression system found that TPMT.21 enzymatic activity was undetectable (18708949). An additional in vitro study using a yeast expression system found that TPMT.21 enzymatic activity is decreased by 75% compared to TPMT.1 and molecular modeling suggests that TPMT.21 alters the co-factor binding site (18602085). Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*21-defining SNV, c.205C>G (p.L69V), impairs protein function.

NAT2*6 is assigned decreased function based on definitive evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*6 allele as "slow" . Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". NAT2*6 is characterized by the core SNVs c.590G>A (p.R197Q) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly exhibited increased function in vivo and in vitro. The functional SNV c.590G>A (p.R197Q) has been studied for more than 25 years and has shown decreased N-acetylation activity in numerous clinical and in vitro studies using various substrates. Clinical studies have demonstrated that individuals homozygous for NAT2*6 or heterozygous for NAT2*6 in combination with another decreased function allele exhibit decreased acetylation activity toward caffeine metabolites (8102597, 7668286, 9891107, 10744128, 20801937, 22970273, 24221535, 28653770), isoniazid (2376572, 9154882, 9321764, 19336229, 35928262), sulphonamides (1872889, 15032315, 16570187, 18167504, 21946899), and dapsone (10762005). Individuals homozygous for NAT2*6 or heterozygous for NAT2*6 in combination with another decreased function allele exhibit increased hydralazine exposure (24702251, 31257615). Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. In vitro studies have shown that NAT2*6 exhibits decreased thermostability and decreased protein expression (11337936, 17434923, 8081359) as well as decreased acetylation toward various substrates (7669073, 33815485). Several studies indicate the existence of an ultra-slow acetylator phenotype in individuals homozygous for the NAT2*6 allele compared with other NAT2 decreased function alleles (29018032, 28653770, 24221535, 33815485, 7668286, 22970273, 28752039, 16570187, 28516247).

CYP2B6*17 (c.76A>T, p.T26S; c.83A>G, p.D28G; c.85C>A+c.86G>C, p.R29T) – Normal function: CYP2B6*17 is assigned normal function. A study evaluating efavirenz content in hair samples among HIV patients taking efavirenz for more than three months found that long term efavirenz exposure was not impacted by the CYP2B6*17 allele (26655325). Additionally, CYP2B6*17 had similar enzyme activity in vitro using cDNA expression systems compared to the *1 reference (16272958, 30201214, 32238417), but decreased activity for methadone (35538637). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on limited evidence.
CYP2D6*58 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.
CYP2D6*73 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

NAT2*55 is assigned unknown function based on a lack of evidence. NAT2*55 is characterized by the core SNVs c.51C>G (p.N17K) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.51C>G (p.N17K). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.51C>G (p.N17K) is neutral for protein function.

CYP2C19*28 is assigned normal function based on limited in vitro and computational evidence. An in vitro study found CYP2C19*28 had similar intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with a mephenytoin substrate (25001882). A computational analysis including splice site, mFold and nonsynonymous mutation analysis, did not find a deleterious impact on enzyme function (20712527). Although CYP2C19*28 was found to have increased intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with a clopidogrel substrate in vitro (25001882), the experts did not find this result convincing as computational analysis and in vitro data with mephenytoin did not support increased function. Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically similar to phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.
CYP2D6*103 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

NAT2*58 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*58 is characterized by the core SNVs c.346G>A (p.G116S), c.590G>A (p.R197Q) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no experimental evidence describing function for c.346G>A (p.G116S). Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.346G>A (p.G116S) impairs protein function, which is supported by published in silico analysis (29273096). c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*58 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.346G>A and c.590G>A are uncertain

CYP2D6*124 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*124 is defined by a deletion resulting in a frameshift and yielding a nonfunctional protein. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence.

NAT2*64 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*64 is characterized by the core SNVs c.590G>A (p.R197Q), c.796G>A (p.V266M) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.796G>A (p.V266M). Three out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict c.796G>A (p.V266M) impairs protein function. c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*64 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.796G>A and c.590G>A are uncertain

CYP2C9*58 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. CYP2C9*58 was first described in a patient requiring a low weekly warfarin dose of 9mg (25075423). No variant was observed in VKORC1 or CYP4F2 in this patient and a subsequent in silico study showed changes in hydrogen bonding which may affect the secondary structure of the protein. In vitro studies found CYP2C9*58 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity with multiple substrates (25142737, 25075423, 25884291). Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2D6*123 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate data describing function of this allele. *123 has the same core SNPs as *41 plus 2870T>C (L302P); this is the only allele with the latter and there does not seem to be additional functional information. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to no available evidence.
CYP2C9*73 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

CYP2D6*143 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*143 is defined by a premature stop codon predicted to yield truncated protein and thus be nonfunctional. A subject sequenced as CYP2D6*143/*144 had a risperidone/9-hydroxy-risperidone ratio that was higher (indicating lower activity) compared to that found in subjects with two no function alleles(33998274). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function.

CYP2C9*33 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies using different cell systems found CYP2C9*33 demonstrated decreased clearance for numerous substrates compared to wildtype and similar clearance compared to CYP2C9*3 which definitively has no function (23752738, 18971529, 23844998, 24118918, 25142737, 25951663, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 27163851, 19541829, 24077631). Although one patient with CYP2C9 *1/*33 genotype had their blood pressure lowered with losartan, the experts did not find this result convincing due to the small sample size (18971529). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

CYP2C9*60 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. CYP2C9*60 was first described in a patient requiring a low daily warfarin dose of 1.5mg (26255664). In vitro studies found CYP2C9*60 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from decreased to similar activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (25142737, 26255664, 25884291). Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2C9*43 is assigned no function based on limited evidence in in vitro studies. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*43 demonstrated markedly decreased clearance compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25142737, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25951663, 27163851, 23844998). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function or decreased function alleles.

CYP2C9*44 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*44 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype (24118918, 25476996, 25144335, 25832633, 25951663, 25884291, 25924705, 23844998, 24077631). Although two in vitro studies showed CYP2C9*44 had similar activity compared to wildtype, the experts did not find these results convincing as in these studies CYP2C9*2 which definitively has decreased function, demonstrated increased or similar activity compared to wildtype in these studies (25142737, 27163851). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2D6*13 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2D6*13 was first described in a subject demonstrating markedly decreased dextromethorphan metabolism (8554938). CYP2D6*13 is a CYP2D7-CYP2D6 hybrid allele resulting in a premature stop codon and yielding a truncated nonfunctional enzyme. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

SLCO1B1*23 is assigned no function based on limited evidence in heterozygous patients and in vitro studies. A clinical study of 1 heterozygous carrier of *23 showed markedly decreased high-dose methotrexate clearance compared to wildtype, slower than a *5/*5 patient (22147369). In vitro uptake was severely diminished compared to wildtype (22147369). Computational activity predictions support the in vitro and in vivo data (22147369).Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with limited evidence.

SLCO1B1*39 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. SLCO1B1*39 is defined by c.388A>G (p.N130D, defining *37) and c.757C>T (p.R253X). The latter results in the generation of a premature stop codon and expression of a truncated protein while c.388A>G is associated with normal SLCO1B1 function. The nonsense variant occurs upstream of the known "no function" variant (c.1463G>C, defining *9 and *31), suggesting that loss of this region of the protein alters function. Additionally, SLCO1B1 loss-of-function has been implicated in the pathogenesis of Rotor syndrome and SLCO1B1*39 has been identified in a homozygous or compound heterozygous state in three cases (32082363, 36964102). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors (VEPs) tested by CPIC staff predict that c.388A>G is neutral for protein function. Four out of four VEPs capable of making predictions for nonsense variants predict that c.757C>T impairs protein function.

CYP2C9*8 is assigned decreased function based on definitive evidence in heterozygous patients and in vitro studies. Ten CYP2C9*1/*8 genotype patients on a stable warfarin dose had 28% lower S-warfarin clearance compared to wildtype patients which correlated with the in vitro kinetic study demonstrating the CYP2C9*8 protein had 30% reduced warfarin clearance compared to wildtype (22378156). The CYP2C9*8 allele was associated with decreased phenytoin metabolism in 19 subjects (CYP2C9*1/*8, CYP2C9*8/*9, CYP2C9*8/*11, or CYP2C9*5/*8) compared to wildtype subjects based on urinary excretion of a phenytoin metabolite after a single 300mg dose (16220110). Four CYP2C9*1/*8 subjects had decreased diclofenac metabolism compared to wildtype subjects based on the urinary metabolite ratio after a single 50mg dose (23959274). One in vitro study showed CYP2C9*8 had decreased mRNA expression compared to wildtype, resulting in decreased function and metabolism of substrates (23376925). Decreased metabolism of multiple substrates compared to wildtype was demonstrated in a series of in vitro studies (23752738, 27199745, 23844998, 24118918, 25142737, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25924705, 27163851, 25951663, 24077631). Although an in vitro study showed similar diclofenac metabolism compared to wildtype (22378156), the experts did not find this conflicting because four CYP2C9*1/*8 subjects exhibited decreased diclofenac metabolism as previously described (23959274). Additionally, the experts did not deem two in vitro studies where CYP2C9*8 demonstrated similar to increased enzyme activity compared to wildtype (28758225, 15284535) as conflicting evidence because both studies used the same system, and it could not be ruled out that these results which differed from the majority of in vitro studies were due to the specific system. Although six CYP2C9*1/*8 subjects had similar acenocoumarol metabolite plasma concentrations compared to wildtype subjects 8 and 24 hours after a single 8mg dose (21811894), the experts did not deem this result as conflicting evidence based on the small sample size. Similarly, two CYP2C9 *1/*8 subjects had a similar urinary losartan-metabolite ratio compared to wildtype after a single 25mg dose and the experts did not deem this result as convincing conflicting evidence based on the small sample size (15289788). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on definitive evidence.

CYP2D6*55 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*55 had decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with multiple substrates (18784265, 24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence.
CYP2D6*122 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

NAT2*37 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*37 is characterized by the core SNVs c.590G>A (p.R197Q), c.638C>T (p.P213L) and c.803G>A. c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no experimental evidence describing function for c.638C>T (p.P213L). Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.638C>T (p.P213L) impairs protein function, which is supported by published in silico analysis (29273096). c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*37 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. Despite this, it remains unknown whether there are any combinatorial effects associated with c.638C>T and c.590G>A.

CYP2D6*70 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*70 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (24647041, 30366777) but consensus among experts was uncertain function.
CYP2D6*121 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

CYP2D6*18 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. CYP2D6*18 is defined by a 9 base insertion yielding a nonfunctional enzyme and has been described in subjects demonstrating markedly decreased metabolism of sparteine and debrisoquine (8946471, 10471060). Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2D6*18 had decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with various substrates (8946471, 18784265, 24647041, 30366777). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on moderate evidence.

CYP2D6*37 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2D6*37 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with tamoxifen, bufuralol, dextromethorphan, and primaquine substrates (24647041, 30366777). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse and not consistent among substrates. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2D6*50 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. CYP2C19*26 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence in a heterozygous subject and in vitro studies. CYP2C19*26 was first described in a subject also carrying the CYP2C19*2 allele and demonstrated decreased omeprazole metabolism compared to wildtype and CYP2C19 *1/*2 or CYP2C19*1/*3 subjects based on the plasma metabolite ratio. This result was correlated with an in vitro study which showed CYP2C19*26 demonstrated decreased intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with mephenytoin and omeprazole substrates (19661214). An additional in vitro study using a different cell system similarly found CYP2C19*26 demonstrated decreased intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with mephenytoin and clopidogrel substrates (25001882). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

NAT2*72 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*72 is defined by the core SNVs c.67A>C (p.T23P) and c.341T>C (p.I114T). c.341T>C (p.I114T) defines NAT2*5 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. In vitro studies also show that NAT2*5 (which contains the core SNV c.341T>C, p.I114T) is subjected to enhanced protein degradation by the proteasome (15564878). Thus, NAT2*72 would presumably be degraded in the same manner. Additionally, five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.341T>C (p.I114T) impairs protein function. There is no literature describing function for c.67A>C. Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.67A>C (p.T23P) impairs protein function. NAT2*72 is assigned decreased function based on c.341T>C. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.341T>C and c.67A>C are uncertain.

NAT2*39 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*39 is characterized by the core SNVs c.590G>A (p.R197Q), c.803G>A (p.R268K), and c.838G>A (p.V280M). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. Published in silico modeling predicts that c.838G>A (p.V280M) impairs protein function based on the exposure of hydrophobic residues that may facilitate protein aggregation and/or targeted protein degradation (19909761). NAT2*39 was identified in one patient (genotyped NAT2*4/*39) in a NAT2 phenotyping study using isoniazid. This patient was classified as an intermediate acetylator and this study showed lower acetyl-INH/INH ratios for intermediate acetylators compared to rapid acetylators, defined as individuals homozygous for NAT2 increased function alleles *1 or *4 or heterozygous NAT2*1/*4 (24533708). Four out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.838G>A (p.V280M) impairs protein function, which is supported by published in silico predictions (27984508). c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*39 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. Despite this, it remains unknown whether there are any combinatorial effects associated with c.838G>A and c.590G>A.

CYP2C9*9 is assigned normal function based on strong evidence in heterozygous patients, homozygous patients, and in vitro studies. Twenty-one subjects with CYP2C9 *1/*9 genotype and three subjects with CYP2C9 *9/*9 genotype had similar urinary excretion of a phenytoin metabolite compared to CYP2C9 *1/*1 subjects following a phenytoin 300mg dose, demonstrating CYP2C9*9 is not associated with decreased phenytoin metabolism (16220110). Seven subjects with CYP2C9 *1/*9 genotype had similar acenocoumarol plasma concentrations 8 and 24 hours after an 8mg dose compared to CYP2C9 *1/*1 subjects (21811894). Seven patients on a stable dose of warfarin with CYP2C9 *1/*9 genotype had a similar maintenance warfarin dose compared to CYP2C9 *1/*1 patients (20716240). In vitro studies demonstrated CYP2C9*9 intrinsic clearance or enzyme activity was similar compared to wildtype using warfarin and tolbutamide substrates (23752738, 15284535). Although one in vitro study showed CYP2C9*9 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity with a 7-ethoxycoumarin substrate compared to wildtype (28758225), the experts did not find this result convincing as no clinical difference was seen in patients taking acenocoumarol or warfarin, or in another in vitro study using a warfarin substrate as previously described. Although one study using the polyphen computational program to predict the functional effect of the SNP defining CYP2C9*9 predicted the substitution as possibly damaging to enzyme function, the authors also noted that this effect has not been shown in clinical phenotypic studies (19164093). Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function with an activity value of 1 based on strong evidence.
