NAT2*19 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*19 allele as "slow" . Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". NAT2*19 is characterized by the core SNVs c.190C>T (p.R64W) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly exhibited increased function in vivo and in vitro. In vitro studies of c.190C>T (p.R64W) have shown decreased protein expression, decreased thermostability, and significantly decreased N-acetyltransferase activity toward sulfamethazine (12222688). An additional in vitro study found that amino acid substitutions of arginine 64 to alanine, lysine, methionine, or glutamine (the latter of which defines NAT2*14) significantly decreased NAT2 protein stability and N-acetyltransferase activity toward p-aminosalicylic acid and all but p.R64K showed decreased N-acetyltransferase activity toward sulfamethazine (9173883). These results highlight the importance of the p.R64 residue for proper NAT2 catalytic activity. Published in silico modeling predicts that c.190C>T (p.R64W) decreases NAT2 protein stability, decreases flexibility and hydrogen bond capacity, and increases hydrophobicity and aromaticity resulting in a loss of electrostatic interactions (18680467). Similarly, six out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.190C>T (p.R64W) impairs NAT2 protein function.

name: c.12242C>T
findings: None

NAT2*56 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. NAT2*56 is characterized by the core SNVs c.58G>T (p.D20Y), c.590G>A (p.R197Q) and c.803G>A (p.R268K). c.803G>A (p.R268K) defines NAT2*4, formerly considered the reference allele, which has repeatedly shown increased function in vivo and in vitro. There is no literature describing function for c.58G>T (p.D20Y). Eight out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.58G>T (p.D20Y) impairs protein function. c.590G>A (p.R197Q) defines NAT2*6 which has been extensively studied, exhibiting decreased N-acetyltransferase activity both in vivo and in vitro. Similarly, seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.590G>A (p.R197Q) impairs protein function. Thus, NAT2*56 is assigned decreased function based on c.590G>A. Despite this, the combinatorial effects associated with c.58G>T and c.590G>A are uncertain.

NAT2*48 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. The NAT gene nomenclature committee has historically assigned acetylation activity to alleles as either "rapid" or "slow", classifying the NAT2*48 allele as "slow" . Under the CPIC allele function terminology, slow acetylation alleles are categorized as "Decreased Function". In vitro evidence demonstrates that NAT2*48 N-acetylation activity is undetectable when using sulfamethazine as a substrate. NAT2*48 mRNA expression levels are comparable to NAT2*4, but protein expression is undetectable in mammalian cells (17264801, 17434923). Despite the undetectable enzymatic activity, the authors concluded that NAT2*48 exhibits decreased function rather than no function. Similarly, the committee could not come to consensus on a call of no function due to the lack of in vivo data supporting a complete loss of function. Published in silico modeling suggests that c.364G>A (p.D122N) disrupts the catalytic triad and the stability of the active site loop (18680467). Eight out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.364G>A (p.D122N) impairs protein function.

SLCO1B1*19 is assigned uncertain function based on no in vitro evidence and limited clinical data. One clinical study showed 1 heterozygous subject treated with pravastatin had similar pharmacokinetic parameters to subjects with wildtype alleles (15226675). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

SLCO1B1*24 is assigned uncertain function based on no in vitro evidence and limited clinical data. One clinical study showed 2 heterozygous subjects treated with high-dose methotrexate had similar methotrexate clearance to subjects with wildtype alleles (22147369). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

NUDT15*6 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. In vitro studies using a bacterial expression system found that NUDT15.6 exhibits decreased enzymatic activity using TGTP or TdGTP as substrates compared to NUDT15.1 (26878724, 33753169). Additionally, NUDT15.6 exhibits reduced protein thermostability compared to NUDT15.1 (26878724, 33753169). The destabilizing effects of c.55_56insGAGTCG (p.V18_V19insGV) were confirmed using an experimentally derived crystal structure of NUDT15.6 (33753169). NUDT15*1/*6 patients have been shown to tolerate lower doses of thiopurines (32221476, 34665987) and one patient genotyped NUDT15*3/*6 tolerated a significantly lower thiopurine dose (32873882) compared to NUDT15*1/*1 patients. Patients carrying a NUDT15*6 allele also exhibit increased incidence of adverse reactions and increased therapy interruption compared to NUDT15*1/*1 patients (28903549, 30728528, 37454061, 34665987, 36873097, 30806694). NUDT15*6 is characterized by the in-frame insertion c.55_56insGAGTCG (p.V18_V19insGV), which is predicted to impair protein function by CADD and PhD-SNPg

SLCO1B1*47 is assigned no function due to no literature describing function of this allele. It contains 3 variants, rs2306283, rs4149056, both included in the no function *15 allele and rs72559747. One in vitro study found normal protein expression and transport for the rs72559747 variant (33658230). Based on evidence supporting the function of rs4149056C>T (11477075, 32961594, 21245207), SLCO1B1*47 is assigned no function with limited evidence.
SLCO1B1*48 is assigned no function due to evidence supporting a partial gene deletion (22232210). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function due to limited evidence.

CYP2C9*21 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In one in vitro study, CYP2C9*21 demonstrated no enzyme activity compared to wildtype with a warfarin and tolbutamide substrate (23752738). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to insufficient evidence.

NUDT15*16 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. An in vitro study expressed NUDT15*16 in HEK293 cells and found that NUDT15.16 protein levels were significantly decreased compared to NUDT15.1, and NUDT15*16 increased thiopurine sensitivity in cells (32094176). Seven out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the NUDT15*16-defining SNV, c.88C>T (p.L30V), impairs protein function.

name: c.11314C>T
findings: PS4_Sup, PM5_Sup, PP3_Mod

name: c.11315G>A
findings: PS4_Mod, PP1_St, PP3_Mod, BS2_Mod

name: c.12028G>A
findings: PS4_Sup, BP4

name: c.13994_13995delTCinsCT
findings: PS2_PM6_Sup, PM1_Sup

CYP2C9*20 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In one in vitro study, CYP2C9*20 demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with a warfarin and tolbutamide substrate (23752738). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to insufficient evidence.

name: *3B
findings: TPMT*3B is assigned no function based on definitive evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals heterozygous for TPMT*3B exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (9246020, 23585236, 33928426, 31005613, 12217596, 16691038), while erythrocytes from individuals heterozygous for TPMT*3B in combination with another no function allele exhibited low enzymatic activity (31005613). Patients carrying TPMT*3B alleles exhibited increased plasma concentrations of TGNs, increased DNA-incorporated thioguanine, decreased plasma concentrations of methylated metabolites, and tolerated lower doses of thiopurines (23585236, 29387964, 33928426, 33205673). In vitro studies demonstrate that TPMT.3B exhibited decreased protein expression, decreased protein stability, and enzymatic activity is decreased by 75-100% relative to TPMT.1 in yeast (9177237, 8644731) and mammalian (16220112, 18708949, 8561894) cells. TPMT.3B decreased protein expression is attributed to rapid degradation by the proteasome (16220112, 9177237) as well as TPMT.3B aggresome formation (15967990). Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*3B-defining SNV, c.460G>A (p.A154T), impairs protein function.

TPMT*7 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*7 exhibited intermediate activity (9711875, 9831928). In vitro studies using mammalian cell systems found that TPMT.7 enzymatic activity is either comparable (16220112) or decreased by 90% (13679074, 1870894) relative to TPMT.1, and protein expression levels are comparable to TPMT.1 in mammalian (16220112, 18708949) and yeast cells (13679074). Additionally, studies agree that TPMT.7 exhibited decreased affinity for 6-TG in vitro (13679074, 18708949). One out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predicts that the TPMT*7-defining SNV, c.681T>G (p.H227Q), impairs protein function. Despite conflicting in vitro evidence, the experts agreed to assign no function based on the available clinical data.

TPMT*8 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. Clinically, erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*8 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (9931346, 27564568, 39182670). Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*8/*33 or TPMT*8/*8 also exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (33118454), while individuals genotyped TPMT*3A/*8 or TPMT *3C/*8 exhibited decreased activity by 16% and 42%, respectively, relative to normal metabolizers. Despite these observations, thiopurine dose intensity is not significantly different between individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*8 and TPMT*1/*1 (27564568, 29785012, 34412101). In vitro studies in mammalian cell systems found that TPMT.8 protein expression is comparable (16220112) or increased (18708949) compared to TPMT.1. These results are corroborated by a study which found TPMT.8 protein abundance is comparable to TPMT.1 (29785012). Enzyme kinetic analysis indicates that TPMT.8 activity is decreased by 28% relative to TPMT.1 (16220112), while another study found that TPMT.8 enzymatic activity is not statistically different from TPMT.1 and that TPMT.8 has increased affinity for 6-TG (18708949). Recombinant TPMT.8 expressed and purified from bacterial cells exhibited identical biophysical characteristics to TPMT.1 (30023734). Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*8-defining SNV, c.644G>A (p.R215H), does not impair protein function. Despite conflicting in vitro evidence, the experts agreed to assign decreased function based on the available clinical data.

CYP2C9*25 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. CYP2C9*25 is defined by a deletion which leads to a frameshift resulting in an early stop codon and truncated protein. This was demonstrated in an in vitro study where CYP2C9*25 had similar mRNA levels compared to wildtype but no protein expression (16788382). CYP2C9*25 was not included in subsequent in vitro studies because it is it is a null allele resulting in no protein formation (23752738). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal and decreased function alleles.

NUDT15*10 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. While there is no literature describing function for NUDT15*10, c.2T>C is predicted to disrupt protein translation due to the loss of the canonical start codon, and there is no alternative start codon available. Three out of seven variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the NUDT15*10 SNV (c.2T>C) impairs protein function.

CYP2C9*30 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*30 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype. These studies used different cell systems and substrates included warfarin, tolbutamide, diclofenac, losartan, and glimepiride (23752738, 19541829, 16788382). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2C9*36 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C9*36 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from markedly decreased to markedly increased compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25142737, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25951663, 25924705, 27163851, 23844998). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.
12893984: testosterone, nifedipine (in vitro)
15179407: midalozam (in vivo)

CYP2C9*42 is assigned no function based on limited evidence in one heterozygous patient and in vitro studies. One subject with CYP2C9*1/*42 genotype exhibited twice the AUC and less than half the oral clearance compared to wildtype subjects based on tolbutamide plasma concentrations after one 500mg dose (25614704). Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*42 demonstrated markedly decreased clearance compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25142737, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25951663, 25884291, 25924705, 27163851, 23844998, 25614704). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function or decreased function alleles.

CYP2C9*47 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C9*47 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from decreased to increased compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25142737, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25924705, 25951663, 27163851, 23844998). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.
14515058: midazolam (in vitro)

CYP2C9*49 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C9*49 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from decreased to increased compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25142737, 25144335, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25951663, 27163851, 23844998). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

CYP2C9*50 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. Multiple in vitro studies found CYP2C9*50 demonstrated decreased clearance compared to wildtype for multiple substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25476996, 25144335, 25951663, 25832633, 25884291, 27163851, 25142737, 25924705, 23844998). Therefore, consensus among experts was decreased function with an activity value of 0.5 based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2C9*51 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C9*51 demonstrated enzyme activity ranging from decreased to increased compared to wildtype with various substrates (24077631, 24118918, 25142737, 25144335, 25951663, 25476996, 25832633, 25884291, 25924705, 27163851, 23844998). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

name: c.3257G>A
findings: 9199552: positive IVCT; 11260227: positive IVCT; 12411788: positive IVCT; 16163667: positive IVCT; 15201141: Similar to that observed for MH mutations in RYR1, the R1086H MH mutation in the 1S-subunit of the skeletal muscle DHPR increases the sensitivity of the calcium release mechanism to activation by both pharmacological (caffeine) and endogenous (voltage sensor) activators. Threshold for caffeine-induced calcium release is approximately fivefold lower in R1086H- expressing myotubes. Increased "sensitivity" of RYR1 to activation by caffeine may be a consequence of an elevated resting calcium level in R1086H- expressing myotubes. The data suggest that the intracellular III-IV linker of the DHPR acts as a negative regulatory module for release channel activation and that the R1086H MH mutation in 1S disrupts this critical negative allosteric regulatory mechanism.

CYP2C9*70 is assigned uncertain funciton due to inadequate evidence. The nonsynonymous variants present in CYP2C9*70 are predicted to be deleterious in SIFT and and benign in Polyphen predictions. There are no in vitro or clinical studies reported. Consensus was not achieved among experts and therefore uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2C9*71 is assigned uncertain function due to limited literature describing function of this allele. This allele has the same variants as *10 (uncertain function) and *12 (decreased function) but no literature supporting the function for *71. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*10 (c.62A>T, p.Q21L and c.64C>T, p.R22C) - Uncertain function: CYP2B6*10 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems (21746968, 30201214) demonstrated decreased enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference. However, consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence as it could not be determined if diplotypes containing this allele would be phenotypically distinct from diplotypes comprising two normal function alleles.

TPMT*42 is assigned no function based on strong evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*42 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (31005613, 32865889), while erythrocyte from an individual genotyped TPMT*3A/*42 exhibited low enzymatic activity (31005613, 32865889).The TPMT*42-defining SNV, c.95_96insA, results in a frameshift of the coding sequence and the creation of a premature stop codo, which is predicted to undergo nonsense-mediated decay. The variant effect predictors, CADD and PhD-SNPg, predict that c.95_96insA (p.Trp33ValfsTer26) impairs protein function.

CYP2B6*21 (c.1282C>A, p.P428T) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*21 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems found no or severely decreased enzyme activity for CYP2B6*21 (20517174, 21746968, 16272958, 30201214). However, there was no in vivo data available or data in human liver microsomes. Therefore, the consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence.

TPMT*28 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*3A/*28 exhibited very low enzymatic activity (20945351). The patient exhibited a plasma concentration of 6-TGN above the therapeutic range and MMP was undetectable (20945351). Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*28-defining SNV, c.349G>C (p.G117R), impairs protein function.

CYP2B6*3 (c.777C>A, p.S259R) - Uncertain function: CYP2B6*3 is assigned uncertain function. In vitro studies using cDNA expression systems demonstrated that CYP2B6*3 had similar enzyme activity or slightly decreased activity compared to the *1 reference (12642465, 30201214). This discrepancy is probably due to differences in the in vitro expression systems and substrates used. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

CYP2B6*12 (c.296G>A, p.G99E) - No function: CYB2B6*12 is assigned no function. Multiple in vitro studies using human liver microsomes (15190123) and cDNA expression systems (20517174, 21746968, 30201214) found that CYP2B6*12 has no protein expression or enzyme activity compared to the *1 reference with various substrates. Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence.

CYP2B6*23 (c.1375A>G, p.M459V) – Uncertain function: CYP2B6*23 is assigned uncertain function. One in vitro cDNA expression study found evidence of higher expression which did not cause a change in activity (30201214), while another study showed slightly increased activity (21746968). Therefore, the consensus among experts was uncertain function based on inadequate evidence.

TPMT*29 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. The TPMT*29 -defining SNV, c.2T>C (p.M1T), is predicted to disrupt protein translation due to the loss of the canonical start codon, and there is no alternative start codon available. An individual genotyped TPMT*1/*29 was predicted to be an intermediate metabolizer (22447118). Five out of eight variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.2T>C (p.M1T) impairs protein function.

NUDT15*3 is assigned no function based on definitive evidence. In vitro studies using a bacterial expression system have found that NUDT15.3 enzymatic activity is significantly decreased or undetectable (26878724, 28659275, 33753169) and protein stability is decreased (29519865, 27530327, 33753169). In mammalian cells, NUDT15.3 protein expression is decreased (32094176, 38246006) and the protein was shown to be actively degraded by the proteasome (27530327). Additionally, NUDT15*1/*3 or NUDT15*3/*3 cell lines exhibit increased levels of DNA-TG and increased sensitivity to 6-MP compared to NUDT15*1/*1 cell lines (34636169). These data are supported by published structural analysis which indicates that the NUDT15*3 SNV, c.415C>T (p.R139C), disrupts the α-helix loop and the base of the substrate binding pocket, and may form a disulfide bond with residue 140, decreasing enzymatic activity (28088792). While the formation of a disulfide bond was not observed, the destabilizing effects of c.415C>T (p.R139C) were confirmed using an experimentally derived crystal structure of NUDT15.3 (33753169). Four out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.415C>T (p.R139C) impairs protein function. Mouse models of NUDT15*1/*3 or NUDT15*3/*3 are more susceptible to MP treatment and exhibit increased cytopenia and bone marrow failure compared to NUDT15*1/*1 mice (31645647). Additionally, HSCs derived from the bone marrow of NUDT15*3/*3 mice exhibit decreased viability and an upregulation of genes associated with DNA damage response and the cell cycle (37488179). The level of dose-normalized DNA-TG is increased in NUDT15*1/*3 or NUDT15*3/*3 patients (26878724, 34480209, 28445187, 33481917, 39688234) relative to NUDT15*1/*1 patients. Lastly, NUDT15*1/*3 or NUDT15*3/*3 patients tolerate significantly lower thiopurine doses compared to NUDT15*1/*1 patients (26878724, 33481917, 32598049, 28903549, 32308129, 29720126, 32873882, 36858569, 35156873, 32221476, 31507415, 27577869, 27416873, 26033531, 39688234) and experience an increased incidence of adverse reactions and/or therapy interruption (30728528, 37454061, 28903549, 36858569).

CYP2C19*7 is assigned no function based on limited evidence in a heterozygous subject. CYP2C19*7 is defined by a splicing defect and was described in a subject demonstrating decreased metabolism of mephenytoin based on urine metabolite concentrations after three separate phenotyping studies (10411572). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically distinct from phenotypes comprising normal function and decreased function alleles.

CYP2C19*18 is assigned normal function based on limited evidence. Four in vitro studies found CYP2C19*18 demonstrated similar intrinsic clearance compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, omeprazole, and clopidogrel substrates (17455109, 18312490, 25001882, 21325430). Therefore, consensus among experts was normal function based on limited evidence as it was determined the phenotypes containing this allele would be clinically similar to phenotypes comprising normal function alleles.

CYP2C19*1 is assigned normal function based on definitive evidence. CYP2C19*1 has been studied for more than 20 years and is the most frequently observed allele (7487078, 32602114). CYP2C19*1 has repeatedly demonstrated normal function with numerous substrates and its association with normal function is well-established (22027650, 29325448, 33237321).

CYP2C19*29 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate evidence. In vitro studies found CYP2C19*29 demonstrated modest decreased enzyme activity compared to wildtype with mephenytoin, omeprazole, and voriconazole substrates (26153442, 30464555). One in vitro study found increased clearance relative to wild-type for nebivolol (29098786). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse and conflicting and it was not convincing the magnitude of decreased activity was clinically significant. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

TPMT*30 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. An in vitro study using a mammalian cell expression system found that TPMT.30 exhibited decreased affinity for 6-TG and 90% decreased enzymatic activity relative to TPMT.1 (18708949). One out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predicts that the TPMT*30-defining SNV, c.106G>A (p.G36S), impairs protein function. Of note, TPMT*30 was originally designated TPMT*24 (23407052).

CYP2C19*39 is assigned uncertain function due to inadequate data. Currently, there is no published evidence describing the function of the CYP2C19*39 allele. One in vitro study for 12718A>C (E122A) determined the metabolic activities towards CEC(3-cyano-7-ethoxycoumarin) O-deethylation and concluded decreased function for this variant (21692664) and another study found the Clint for S-mephenytoin is not significantly different and for omeprazole decreased to 0.27 fold compared to no variant (21325430). Consensus was not achieved among experts as data were sparse and not consistent among substrates. Therefore, uncertain function was assigned.

CYP2C9*7 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence due to conflicting in vitro data and no clinical data. In an in vitro study CYP2C9*7 demonstrated 47% warfarin clearance compared to wildtype, however, similar tolbutamide clearance compared to wildtype was demonstrated in the same study as well as a separate study (23752738, 15284535). Therefore, consensus among experts was not achieved and uncertain function was assigned.

TPMT*31 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. TPMT.31 exhibited decreased protein expression in patient white blood cells (20881512), and erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*31 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity (20881512, 31005613). However, there was no difference in enzymatic activity observed in vitro between recombinant TPMT.1 and TPMT.31 (Vmax=11.1 and 11.8 U/mg, respectively) produced in E coli. (20881512). Five out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*31-defining SNV, c.611T>C (p.I204T), impairs protein function. Despite conflicting in vitro evidence, the experts agreed to assign no function based on the available clinical data.
CYP2C9*57 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

TPMT*32 is assigned uncertain function based on inadequate evidence. Erythrocytes from a patient genotyped TPMT*1/*32 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity and the patient's 6-TGN plasma concentration was 694 pmol/8×108 RBCs after receiving 75 mg/m2 6-MP (23252716). An additional patient genotyped TPMT*1/*32 received 43 mg/m2 6-MP, which is 57% of the standard dose, and the patient exhibited a 6-TGN plasma concentration of 1257 pmol/8×108 RBCs (25564374). Erythrocytes from the second patient exhibited normal TPMT enzymatic activity (25564374). Four out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*32-defining SNV, c.340G>A (p.E114K), impairs protein function.

CYP2C9*3 is assigned no function based on definitive evidence in homozygous and heterozygous patients and in vitro experimental data. CYP2C9*3 has been studied for more than 20 years and its association with impaired function compared to wildtype is well-established (8809086, 29283396, 20150829, 15637526). This allele was previously assigned "decreased function" but after reevaluation of prior and new evidence, CYP2C9*3 is assigned "no function" because its activity is much more decreased compared to CYP2C9*2 for multiple substrates, resulting in the lowest possible designation for the phenotypes observed in homozygous patients (12742136, 10413320, 27179628, 25775139, 19082874, 30088221). Therefore, consensus among experts was no function with an activity value of 0 based on definitive evidence.

TPMT*33 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Erythrocytes from an individual genotyped TPMT*1/*33 exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity and their 6-TGN plasma concentration was 634 pmol/8×108 RBCs after receiving 54 mg/m2 6-MP (23252716). Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*8/*33 exhibited intermediate activity (33118454), however the phase was not determined, and it is possible that these individuals are TPMT*1/*46. Eight out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*33-defining SNV, c.487C>T (p.R163C), impairs protein function.
CYP2D6*102 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. Therefore, consensus among experts was unknown function due to no available evidence.

name: c.3095G>A
findings: None

TPMT*34 is assigned no function based on moderate evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*34 exhibited intermediate activity (23252716, 33118454). One of these patients received 44 mg/m2 thioguanine and exhibited a plasma TGN concentration of 3006 pmol/8×108 RBCs (23252716). Seven out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*34-defining SNV, c.244C>T (p.R82W), impairs protein function.

TPMT*35 is assigned unknown function based on no evidence. There are no in vitro or in vivo functional studies for TPMT*35. Ten out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*35-defining SNV, c.200T>C (p.F67S), impairs protein function.

name: c.3127C>T
findings: PP3_Mod

TPMT*36 is assigned unknown function based on no evidence. There are no in vitro or in vivo functional studies for TPMT*36. Four out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*36-defining SNV, c.595G>A (p.V199I), impairs protein function.

name: c.9676G>C
findings: PS4_Sup

CYP2D6*148 is assigned unknown function. CYP2D6*148 is defined by four SNPs of which two are found on the normal function *2 allele. 77G>A (R26H) is found on the *43 allele and the normal function *46 allele, and the forth SNP (3854G>A, E480K) is present on the normal function *27 allele. Since the functional impact of 993C>G is unknown, consensus among experts was unknown function.

CYP2D6*149 is assigned unknown function. CYP2D6*149 is defined by six SNPs of which five are found on the decreased function *29 allele. 993C>G (D97E) has only been found on *149. Since the functional impact of 993C>G is unknown, consensus among experts was unknown function.
CYP2D6*152 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. CYP2D6*152 is defined by a single SNP that is not present in any other star allele.
CYP2D6*153 is assigned unknown function due to no literature describing function of this allele. CYP2D6*153 is defined by one SNP that is not found on any other star allele.

name: Reference
findings: Individual patients have been reported (PMID 38129972) to carry only one of the two SNPs in the HapB3 haplotype (i.e., c.1236G>A without c.1129-5923C>G), suggesting that the two variants are not in complete linkage disequilibrium. To address this, c.1129-5923C>G SNP is listed separately as this is likely the causal variant leading to decreased function. The HapB3 haplotype definition was also retained for cases where only the c.1236G>A variant is tested (e.g. whole exome sequencing). While CPIC recommends to directly test the causal SNP if possible, in cases where only c.1236G>A is tested or results are available for, it should be clearly stated in the test report that "decreased function" was inferred by detecting the exonic tag SNP, and disclose that in rare cases, the causal decreased function variant c.1129-5923C>G may not be present despite having this tag SNP.

name: A
findings: Although originally classified as a WHO class III/decreased function variant (3446582, 22293322), recent studies show that the catalytic activity and structural stability of G6PD A is similar to that of the WT enzyme (32387609, 1303173). Therefore, consensus among experts was IV/Normal function due to moderate evidence.

name: Reference
clinicalfunctionalstatus: Normal risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss

name: m.951G>A
clinicalfunctionalstatus: uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR1 951G>A is assigned an uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on inadequate evidence. Screening of individuals with aminoglycoside-hearing loss identified this variant in two studies (18830133, 18851951). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss due to insufficient evidence.

name: m.1095T>C
clinicalfunctionalstatus: increased risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR1 1095T>C is assigned an increased risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on moderate evidence. Screening of individuals or probands of individuals with aminoglycoside-hearing looss identified 1095T>C in a number of cases (11079536, 11313749, 15555598, 15841390, 16875663, 21205314). Therefore, consensus among experts was this variant is associated with increased risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on moderate evidence.

NUDT15*19 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. One patient genotyped NUDT15*1/*19 received 150 mg of azathiopurine daily and experienced pancytopenia after 1 week of treatment. However, this patient was also heterozygous for an inactivating TPMT allele (30728528). Four out of seven variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the NUDT15*19-defining SNV (c.3G>C (p.M1I)) impairs protein function. c.3G>C is predicted to disrupt protein translation due to the loss of the canonical start codon, and there is no alternative start codon available.

NUDT15*18 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. One patient identified with the genotype NUDT15*1/*18 received 50 mg of 6-MP daily and experienced pancytopenia after 3 months of treatment. However, this patient was also heterozygous for an inactivating TPMT allele (30728528). NUDT15*18 is characterized by c.221delA, causing a frameshift (p.N74fs), that is predicted to impair protein function by CADD and PhD-SNP6. The deletion occurs in exon 2, which is within the Nudix hydrolase domain, and NUDT15*18 is predicted to produce a protein targeted for nonsense-mediated decay.

name: m.960C>del
clinicalfunctionalstatus: uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR1 960C>del is assigned an uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on inadequate evidence. Screening of individuals with aminoglycoside-hearing loss identified this variant in one study (18851951). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss due to insufficient evidence.

name: m.961T>G
clinicalfunctionalstatus: uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR1 961T<G is assigned an uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on inadequate evidence. One report of normal hearing after aminoglycoside hearing loss in an invidual carrying this variant (18830133). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss due to insufficient evidence.

name: m.961T>del
clinicalfunctionalstatus: uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss
findings: MT-RNR1 961T>del is assigned an uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss based on inadequate evidence. In one study, 961T>del was detected in subjects in all four cohorts (no statistically significant difference in allele frequency between the groups). Pedigree analysis showed that T961del and the hearing loss phenotype did not co-segregate (15917167). Therefore, consensus among experts is that there is uncertain risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss associated with this variant.

functioncomments: 27.7 Vmax/Km; *3/*5 patient has same intolerance to MP as *3/*3 patient
findings: NUDT15*5 is assigned decreased function based on moderate evidence. Patients genotyped NUDT15*1/*5 or NUDT15*3/*5 exhibited increased dose-normalized levels of DNA-TG compared to patients genotyped NUDT15*1/*1 (33481917). Intermediate metabolizers, including those genotyped NUDT15*1/*5, have been shown to tolerate lower thiopurine doses (33481917, 32221476, 36873097, 33504140) and exhibit an increased risk of neutropenia (36873097). One patient genotyped NUDT15*3/*5 exhibited similar thiopurine intolerance to a patient genotyped NUDT15*3/*3 (26878724). In vitro studies using a bacterial expression system found that NUDT15.5 exhibits decreased enzymatic activity and decreased protein stability compared to NUDT15.1 (26878724, 33753169). However, published in silico analysis indicates that the NUDT15*5-defining SNV, c.52G>A (p.V18I) has minimal effect on protein structure and NUDT15.5 exhibits similar thermostability as NUDT15.1 (28088792). Despite these predictions, an experimentally derived crystal structure of NUDT15.5 found that c.52G>A (p.V18I) destabilizes the conserved NUDIX motif (33753169). Five out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.52G>A (p.V18I) impairs protein function. An additional in vitro study found that the NUDT15.5 protein is expressed at similar levels to NUDT15.1 and does not affect 6-MP sensitivity in HEK293 cells (32094176).

NUDT15*8 is assigned decreased function based on limited evidence. One patient genotyped NUDT15*2/*8 was extremely sensitive to thiopurine treatment (28659275). An in vitro study using a bacterial expression system found that NUDT15.8 exhibits decreased enzymatic activity and decreased protein thermostability compared to NUDT15.1 (28659275). An additional in vitro study expressed NUDT15*8 in HEK293 cells and found that protein expression and thiopurine sensitivity were comparable to cells expressing NUDT15*1 (32094176). Seven out of nine variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the NUDT15*8-defining SNV, c.103A>G (p.K35E), impairs protein function.

TPMT*37 is assigned no function based on strong evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals genotyped TPMT*1/*37 exhibited intermediate activity (24710034, 33118454). The TPMT*37-defining SNV, c.648T>A (p.C216X), results in the generation of a premature stop codon and the expression of a truncated protein. The premature stop variant occurs upstream of the known "no function" variant (c.719A>G, defining TPMT*3C), suggesting that loss of this region of the protein alters function. Three out of four variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that c.648T>A (p.C216X) impairs protein function.

SLCO1B1*28 is assigned uncertain function based on no in vitro evidence and limited clinical data. One clinical study showed no statistical difference in methotrexate clearance (22147369). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

SLCO1B1*2 is assigned uncertain function based on in vitro studies demonstrating reduced uptake of estrone sulfate, estradiol 17beta-d-glucuronide and rifampin (11477075, 12490595), but no clinical data. Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

SLCO1B1*15 is assigned no function based on strong evidence in heterozygous and homozygous patients, and in vitro studies. SLCO1B1*15 has been studied for more than 15 years and its association with impaired function compared to wildtype is well-established (21245207, 25350695, 31220337). Though in vitro, the *15 allele may have more function than the *5 allele, analyses of pharmacokinetics and statin-associated muscle symptoms are usually performed on the rs4149056 variant included in the *5 and *15 alleles, so the same functional status was assigned for each of these alleles.

SLCO1B1*32 is assigned uncertain function based on no in vitro evidence and limited clinical data. One clinical study showed no statistical difference in methotrexate clearance for one heterozygous patient (22147369). Therefore, consensus among experts was uncertain function due to inadequate evidence.

name: *3C
findings: TPMT*3C is assigned no function based on definitive evidence. Erythrocytes from individuals heterozygous for TPMT*3C exhibited intermediate enzymatic activity, while erythrocytes from individuals homozygous for TPMT*3C or heterozygous for TPMT*3C in combination with another no function allele exhibited low enzymatic activity (9246020, 9931346, 15226673, 23252716, 31005613, 15255798, 14508387, 9695718, 14656901, 9103127, 24322830, 19048245, 11372592, 18021342, 15167635, 9711875, 16952345, 9831928, 10997970, 14985890). Patients carrying TPMT*3C alleles exhibited increased plasma concentrations of TGNs, increased DNA-incorporated thioguanine, decreased plasma concentrations of methylated metabolites, and tolerated lower doses of thiopurines (11304783, 19057372, 33568932, 33205673, 33928426, 29387964, 23585236). In vitro studies demonstrate that TPMT.3C exhibited decreased protein expression, decreased protein stability, and enzymatic activity was decreased by 50-80% relative to TPMT.1 in yeast cells (9177237, 8644731), mammalian cells (16220112, 18708949, 8561894), and patient erythrocytes (9695718). TPMT.3C decreased protein expression is attributed to rapid degradation by the proteasome (16220112). Eight out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*3C-defining SNV, c.719A>G (p.Y240C), impairs protein function.

TPMT*38 is assigned no function based on limited evidence. Two patients were genotyped TPMT*1/*38. One patient with ALL received 26.9-53.8 mg/m2 mercaptopurine, which is 36-72% of the standard dose. The other patient exhibited intermediate TPMT enzymatic activity (25564374). Four out of ten variant effect predictors tested by CPIC staff predict that the TPMT*38-defining SNV, c.514T>C (p.S172P), impairs protein function.

functioncomments: UGT1A1*80 is in very high linkage disequilibrium with *28 and *37. However, the function of the *80 allele alone, in the absence of *28 or *37, is uncertain. If a patient is only tested for *80, decreased function may be inferred.
